translation: O Prophet, fear Allah and do not obey the disbelievers and the hypocrites. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise
commentary:
**يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا**

Surah Al-Ahzab stands as one of the most unique surahs in the Quran, addressing both intimate social legislation and a pivotal military crisis in the early Muslim community. The surah opens with a direct address to the Prophet ﷺ: "O Prophet, be cautious of Allah, and do not obey the disbelievers and the hypocrites."

This opening command carries profound implications. When Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ to have taqwa—to be cautious and aware of Allah—He is not scolding him. Rather, He is instructing him to fear nothing and no one else. The command means: focus only on what Allah ﷻ expects of you, and don't be influenced by what the disbelievers or hypocrites are saying.

This context is crucial. The surah addresses a controversy that would arise regarding the Prophet's ﷺ marriage to Zaynab (RA), the former wife of his adopted son Zayd ibn Harithah (RA). The hypocrites and disbelievers in Medina, along with the Quraysh in Makkah, were already discussing this matter and preparing to use it against the Prophet ﷺ. Allah ﷻ tells him from the outset: don't worry about their opinions or their pressures. Follow only what I reveal to you.

The ayah concludes with the divine names **عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا** —the All-Knowing, the Perfectly Wise. This is significant. Allah ﷻ could have revealed to the Prophet ﷺ not to marry Zaynab (RA), but He didn't. He waited for this situation to unfold so that important lessons could be learned from it. That is the wisdom (hikmah) of Allah ﷻ. He knows all things and orchestrates events with perfect wisdom.

translation: And follow that which is revealed to you from your Lord. Indeed Allah is ever, with what you do, Acquainted
commentary:
**وَٱتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا**

"And follow what has been revealed to you from your Master. Indeed, Allah ﷻ is fully aware of what you do."

The command intensifies: you don't have to follow any external pressures—you must follow only what has been revealed to you. The word **رَبِّكَ** (your Rabb, your Master) is particularly intimate and reassuring. Your Rabb is the One who nurtures you, raises you, cares for you. So follow only what comes from Him.

The ayah shifts to the plural in its ending: **بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ** (what you all do). This extends beyond just the Prophet ﷺ to the entire Muslim community. Allah ﷻ is **خَبِيرًا** —fully informed, completely aware of everything everyone is doing. He knows the true motivations, the hidden intentions, the whispered conversations of the hypocrites. Nothing escapes His knowledge.

translation: And rely upon Allah; and sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs
commentary:
**وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا**

"And continue to place your trust in Allah. And Allah ﷻ is enough as the One who disposes of your affairs."

This is the spiritual anchor for what lies ahead. **تَوَكَّلْ**  means to rely completely on Allah ﷻ, to trust that He will take care of all matters. When the Prophet ﷺ faced criticism over domestic issues while simultaneously dealing with military threats from the Quraysh and treachery from some of the Jewish tribes in Medina, this command reminded him: place all your trust in Allah ﷻ alone.

**وَكِيلًا**  means the One who manages, disposes of, and takes care of all affairs. When a leader faces a domestic controversy, it can destroy the morale of his followers. People look at personal scandals and lose confidence in leadership. But Allah ﷻ says: I am enough to manage your affairs. Trust Me, and I will handle everything—the criticism, the military threats, the internal dissent, all of it.

translation: Allah has not made for a man two hearts in his interior. And He has not made your wives whom you declare unlawful your mothers. And he has not made your adopted sons your [true] sons. That is [merely] your saying by your mouths, but Allah says the truth, and He guides to the [right] way
commentary:
**مَّا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِّن قَلْبَيْنِ فِى جَوْفِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلَ أَزْوَٰجَكُمُ ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى تُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِنْهُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَآءَكُمْ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِكُمْ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَقُولُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِى ٱلسَّبِيلَ**

This remarkable ayah addresses three distinct pre-Islamic Arab customs, but it begins with a profound statement about human nature: "Allah ﷻ did not place two hearts inside the torso of any man."

The word **جَوْف**  specifically refers to the hollow cavity of the torso—the chest and abdomen that contains our organs. One interpretation of this opening relates directly to the marriage situation: a man cannot have two hearts, one that loves his wife and one that doesn't. If genuine love and compatibility are gone from a marriage, they cannot be manufactured. This was the reality between Zayd (RA) and Zaynab (RA)—despite the Prophet's ﷺ recommendation for their marriage, they were not compatible, and forcing something that isn't there only causes more pain.

The ayah then addresses three specific practices:

First, **ٱلظِّهَار** —the reprehensible practice where a husband, in anger or hatred, would say to his wife: "You are to me like my mother's back," essentially declaring her forbidden to him as his mother would be. This was a form of psychological abuse, leaving the woman in limbo—not divorced but not truly married either. Allah ﷻ declares: just because you call your wife your mother doesn't make her your mother. Your mother is your mother, and your wife is your wife. Words don't change reality.

Second, calling adopted children your biological sons. The Arabs had a custom of referring to adopted children as their actual sons, even changing their lineage. Zayd ibn Harithah (RA) lived in the Prophet's ﷺ household and was so beloved that people called him Zayd ibn Muhammad—Zayd, son of Muhammad. But Allah ﷻ declares: no matter how much you love someone, calling them your son doesn't make them your biological son.

**ذَٰلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِكُمْ** (That is just your saying with your mouths)—this is merely casual speech, expressions of emotion, whether from extreme love or extreme hate. But reality is reality. **وَٱللَّهُ يَقُولُ ٱلْحَقَّ** (And Allah ﷻ speaks the truth)—He tells you what is actually real, not just what you feel or express emotionally.

This principle extends beyond these specific cases. Parents sometimes say in anger, "You're not my son anymore!" But he's still their son. Friends dramatically declare, "From today, you're not my friend!" But these emotional declarations don't change the underlying reality of relationships. Islam distinguishes between expressing love, showing respect, and the letter of the law—particularly when it comes to matters like inheritance and marriage restrictions.

translation: Call them by [the names of] their fathers; it is more just in the sight of Allah. But if you do not know their fathers - then they are [still] your brothers in religion and those entrusted to you. And there is no blame upon you for that in which you have erred but [only for] what your hearts intended. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
**ٱدْعُوهُمْ لِـَٔابَآئِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَمَوَٰلِيكُمْ ۚ وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَآ أَخْطَأْتُم بِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِن مَّا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا**

"Call them by their fathers' names—that is closer to justice with Allah ﷻ."

Identity and lineage matter in Islam, not out of tribalism, but because the constitutional framework of Islamic law depends on proper identification. Inheritance laws, marriage restrictions, family responsibilities—all of these are tied to knowing who people really are and where they come from.

The command is clear: maintain people's connection to their biological fathers. When someone is adopted, don't officially change their last name to erase their origin. The child will eventually need to know they were adopted, and having integrity from the beginning prevents more painful revelations later.

But Allah ﷻ is also merciful and practical: **فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ** (But if you don't know who their fathers are)—whether because of war, abandonment, or other tragic circumstances—**فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَمَوَٰلِيكُمْ** (then they are your brothers in religion and your close associates). Give them any name, but not yours as if they were your biological children. They remain full members of the community, your brothers and sisters in faith, worthy of complete respect and care.

**وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَآ أَخْطَأْتُم بِهِۦ** (There is no blame on you for mistakes you've already made)—before this revelation came, people were calling Zayd "ibn Muhammad" out of love and respect. Allah ﷻ forgives those mistakes. **وَلَـٰكِن مَّا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ** (But rather what your hearts intended deliberately)—it's one thing to slip up in speech, quite another to insist on something wrong even after knowing the truth.

The ayah ends with two beautiful names: **غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا**—Extremely Forgiving, Continually Merciful. These are family matters, and they require counseling, wisdom, and patience. Allah ﷻ is merciful with our human complications and emotions, forgiving of our past mistakes, but He also establishes clear guidelines going forward.

translation: The Prophet is more worthy of the believers than themselves, and his wives are [in the position of] their mothers. And those of [blood] relationship are more entitled [to inheritance] in the decree of Allah than the [other] believers and the emigrants, except that you may do to your close associates a kindness [through bequest]. That was in the Book inscribed
commentary:
**ٱلنَّبِىُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُۥٓ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ ۗ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ إِلَّآ أَن تَفْعَلُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا ۚ كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَسْطُورًا**

"The Prophet has more right over the believers than they have over their own selves, and his wives are their mothers."

This ayah addresses the critics head-on. Those who were talking negatively about the Prophet ﷺ regarding the Zaynab (RA) situation needed to be reminded: **ٱلنَّبِىُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ**—the Prophet has a higher priority, more right over the believers than they have over themselves. The Companions (RA) would lay down their lives before they would allow any harm to come to him. They would endure any insult before tolerating disrespect toward him. His judgment, his decisions, take precedence over their own preferences.

This is also mentioned because speaking ill of the Prophet ﷺ reveals one's true state. Only those with genuine faith give him this highest priority. Those who gossip about him expose themselves as hypocrites.

**وَأَزْوَٰجُهُۥٓ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ** (And his wives are their mothers)—this is Allah ﷻ declaring the status of the Mothers of the Believers. Unlike someone merely saying "she's like my mother" in anger, when Allah ﷻ says it, it becomes binding law. From this point forward, whether the Prophet ﷺ remained married to them or not, no other man could marry any of his wives. They became **أُمَّهَـٰتُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (Mothers of the Believers)—a unique, permanent status.

The ayah then clarifies inheritance laws: **وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ** (And blood relatives have more right to inheritance over one another in the Book of Allah ﷻ). The Companions (RA) had such strong brotherhood—the Muhajirun and Ansar were like actual brothers to each other. But when inheritance laws came, Allah ﷻ clarified: just calling someone your brother doesn't make him your heir. Blood relations take priority in inheritance.

**إِلَّآ أَن تَفْعَلُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا** (Except that you may do good to your close friends in an recognized way)—while you're still alive, you can gift property to friends, put a house in someone's name, give away your belongings. That's not inheritance; that's your choice during your lifetime. But once you pass away, the laws of inheritance apply strictly to family members as Allah ﷻ has written.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when We took from the prophets their covenant and from you and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus, the son of Mary; and We took from them a solemn covenant
commentary:
**وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا**

After discussing the marriage contract as something weighty and serious, Allah ﷻ now mentions another weighty contract: the covenant taken from all the prophets.

"And remember when We took from the prophets their covenant, and from you, and from Nuh, and Ibrahim, and Musa, and 'Isa son of Maryam—and We took from them a mighty covenant."

All human beings took a covenant with Allah ﷻ—that is the primordial covenant when all souls acknowledged Him as their Lord. But the prophets took a second, specific covenant: to deliver the message to their people, to call them to truth, to bear witness even when it was difficult.

The word **مِيثَـٰقًا**  refers to a specific type of contract where both parties fully understand the seriousness and the terms. The prophets understood what they were signing up for—the rejection, the persecution, the loneliness. This wasn't a casual agreement.

Notice how Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ first (**وَمِنكَ**—"and from you"), before mentioning the other great messengers chronologically. This honors him and highlights that this surah is about him facing his test, just as those great messengers faced theirs.

When did this covenant happen? Before we came to this earth, in that realm where souls existed. The prophets were given this tremendous responsibility even before their earthly missions began.

translation: That He may question the truthful about their truth. And He has prepared for the disbelievers a painful punishment
commentary:
**لِّيَسْـَٔلَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ عَن صِدْقِهِمْ ۚ وَأَعَدَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا**

"So that He may question the truthful about their truth, and He has prepared for the disbelievers a painful punishment."

The covenant was taken so that on the Day of Judgment, Allah ﷻ will ask the truthful—the prophets who claimed to deliver the message—about their truthfulness. Did you convey what I sent you with? The entire Ummah will be able to compare their testimony against what the prophets say they taught.

And those who disbelieved, who rejected the message that was so clearly delivered, will face a painful punishment. The gravity of the prophetic mission comes into full view: it's not just about this life, but about testimony that will be given on the Day of Judgment.

This ayah concludes the first passage of the surah, which dealt with the internal, domestic matter. Now the surah shifts dramatically to the external crisis—the Battle of the Trench (Ghazwat al-Ahzab).

## Historical Context: The Battle of the Trench

Before examining the next ayahs, understanding the historical context is essential. The Battle of the Trench occurred in the fifth year after the Prophet's ﷺ migration to Medina. Two major battles had already taken place: Badr and Uhud.

Banu Nadir, one of the Jewish tribes in Medina, had been found guilty of treachery—plotting with the Quraysh to undermine the Muslims' security. According to the treaty, their punishment was exile. The entire tribe was expelled from Medina.

One of their leaders, Abu 'Amir, harbored intense hatred for the Muslims after being expelled. He went to the Quraysh in Makkah and proposed a unified assault on Medina. He told them: "We can use your reputation to rally other tribes. Let's combine our forces—offer them spoils of war, promise them victory. We can gather everyone who has any grudge against the Muslims and end this problem once and for all."

The Quraysh agreed. They mobilized a massive coalition force: 12,000 soldiers were assembled and began marching toward Medina.

Medina's geography provided some natural protection. Mountain ranges to the north and south made those borders nearly impassable for a large army—the terrain was volcanic rock, black and slippery, impossible for horses and camels. But the eastern and western borders remained exposed.

Inside Medina, there were only about 3,000 Muslims. The odds were four to one, and that was only counting the army they could see coming. Worse still, Banu Quraydha, another Jewish tribe still living in Medina under treaty, was showing signs of turning against the Muslims as well.

The Prophet ﷺ consulted with his Companions about strategy. Salman al-Farsi (RA), originally from Persia, suggested something the Arabs had never done before: "In Persia, when a small force faces a massive army, we dig a trench at the border. This prevents the large army from flooding in all at once. They can only come through in small numbers, making them manageable."

The suggestion was accepted. In the middle of winter, with food running low and the entire city under threat, the Muslims—led by the Prophet ﷺ himself—began digging a massive trench along the vulnerable border.

It was brutal work. The Companions were starving. Some tied stones to their bellies to suppress their hunger pangs. One Companion came to the Prophet ﷺ saying he hadn't eaten in days. The Prophet ﷺ lifted his own shirt, revealing stones tied to his stomach as well. The leader of the city, the Messenger of Allah ﷻ, was working alongside them, carrying boulders, swinging the pickaxe, suffering the same hunger and cold.

As they worked, they would chant together to keep their spirits up: "We are the ones who have pledged ourselves to Muhammad for jihad that will remain forever."

When they encountered a massive boulder that wouldn't break, they called the Prophet ﷺ. He took the tool and struck it, shattering it completely—a sign of Allah's ﷻ help in their desperate situation.

The trench proved remarkably effective. Horse riders couldn't cross it, warriors couldn't breach it. The coalition army was forced into a stalemate. They surrounded the city, thinking they could wait until the Muslims' supplies ran out and they would surrender.

But then, ominous news arrived: Banu Quraydha, behind the Muslims' lines, had decided to break their treaty and join the attacking forces. Now the Muslims faced armies from two directions—the massive coalition in front and potential betrayal from within.

One strategic Companion went to Banu Quraydha and said, "You know the last two times Quraysh attacked the Muslims, it didn't end well for them. What if there's even a 10% chance the Muslims survive this? Quraysh will go home to Makkah, but you live here. Who do you think the Muslims will come after next?" He planted seeds of doubt, suggesting they demand hostages from the Quraysh as insurance.

Then he went to the Quraysh and told them, "Those people from Medina aren't even sure if they're against Muhammad ﷺ. You know what they're asking for? Hostages. They don't trust you."

Friction developed between the two forces. They were no longer unified in their intent.

Meanwhile, Allah ﷻ sent powerful winds against the besieging army. Tents were blown over, cooking fires were scattered, animals stampeded. Chaos erupted in their camp. After days of this, the coalition—especially the tribes who had joined for easy plunder—decided it wasn't worth it. They packed up and left.

The Muslims, against all odds, were saved. But the internal threat of Banu Quraydha's betrayal would need to be addressed afterward.

translation: O you who have believed, remember the favor of Allah upon you when armies came to [attack] you and We sent upon them a wind and armies [of angels] you did not see. And ever is Allah, of what you do, Seeing
commentary:
**يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ جَآءَتْكُمْ جُنُودٌ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا وَجُنُودًا لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا**

"O you who believe, remember the favor of Allah ﷻ upon you when armies came at you—so We sent against them a wind and armies you could not see. And Allah ﷻ was All-Seeing of what you were doing."

Allah ﷻ commands the believers to remember—**ٱذْكُرُوا۟**—actively recall and reflect on His favor. The favor wasn't just that they survived; it was how they survived. 

**جُنُودٌ** (armies)—plural. Not one army, but multiple armies came against them from different directions, a coordinated assault meant to overwhelm completely.

**فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا** (So We sent against them a wind)—Allah ﷻ takes credit for the wind. This wasn't random weather; this was divine intervention. The wind at just the right time, with just the right intensity, creating chaos in the enemy camp.

**وَجُنُودًا لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا** (And armies you could not see)—the angels. You could see the effects of the wind, the blowing sand and scattered supplies, but the other army—the army of angels who slapped the camels so they ran away, who tipped over the cooking pots starting fires, who sowed confusion—those you couldn't see at all.

**وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا** (And Allah ﷻ was All-Seeing of what you were doing)—He was watching everything. He saw your patience, your struggle, your hunger, your fear. He saw the hypocrites undermining morale. He saw those who remained firm. Everything was in His sight, and His help came at precisely the right moment.

translation: [Remember] when they came at you from above you and from below you, and when eyes shifted [in fear], and hearts reached the throats and you assumed about Allah [various] assumptions
commentary:
**إِذْ جَآءُوكُم مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ وَمِنْ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ وَإِذْ زَاغَتِ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَبَلَغَتِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلْحَنَاجِرَ وَتَظُنُّونَ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلظُّنُونَا۠**

This ayah gives us a remarkable visual perspective, as if we're watching through multiple camera angles.

**إِذْ جَآءُوكُم مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ وَمِنْ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ** (When they came at you from above you and from below you)—we begin with a wide aerial view, a satellite perspective. If you were to look at Medina from above, you'd see armies advancing from two directions: from the elevated ground (the coalition forces descending from the slopes) and from the fortified area of Banu Quraydha behind them. No single soldier in Medina could watch both borders at once, but Allah ﷻ shows us both simultaneously.

Then the camera zooms in dramatically: **وَإِذْ زَاغَتِ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ** (And when eyes turned wild). Now we're looking at a close-up of soldiers' eyes. Not just scared eyes—**زَاغَتْ** means eyes that are darting around in terror, petrified, rolling back. We've gone from a satellite view to an extreme close-up of someone's face, someone who hasn't told anyone he's terrified, but his eyes betray his fear.

**وَبَلَغَتِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلْحَنَاجِرَ** (And hearts reached the throats)—now the camera goes inside, into the chest cavity. You can feel your heart pounding so hard it feels like it's in your throat. Which historian records this? Which cameraman captures what's happening inside someone's chest?

And notice: **ٱلْحَنَاجِرَ**  means the throats—specifically where sound comes from. When your heart is stuck in your throat, you can't speak. Someone asks, "Are you okay?" You can't answer. Your voice is gone.

**وَتَظُنُّونَ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلظُّنُونَا** (And you were thinking about Allah ﷻ various thoughts)—now we zoom even further, into the neurons firing in the brain. What kinds of thoughts were racing through their minds? "I thought Islam was the truth. I thought we were promised victory. The Prophet ﷺ said we would take over the Roman and Persian Empires, that Islam would reach every house. But look at us—we're about to be wiped out. Was this all wrong?"

These weren't the hypocrites thinking this—these were believers. When you're surrounded, starving, terrified, and betrayed, even the faithful can have doubts creep into their minds. Allah ﷻ captures all of it—the wide view, the close-up, the internal state, the hidden thoughts—and preserves it in His Book.

translation: There the believers were tested and shaken with a severe shaking
commentary:
**هُنَالِكَ ٱبْتُلِىَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ زِلْزَالًا شَدِيدًا**

"There and then, the believers were tested, and they were shaken with a severe shaking."

**هُنَالِكَ** —right there, at that precise moment and place. **ٱبْتُلِىَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ** (the believers were tested). If you consider Uhud the most difficult personal test for the Prophet ﷺ, then the Trench was the most difficult communal test for the Muslims as an Ummah.

**وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ زِلْزَالًا شَدِيدًا** (And they were shaken with an intense shaking)—this wasn't a minor tremor. The earth beneath their feet, everything they believed was solid and stable, was shaken. This is the earthquake of fear. This is when your entire worldview is called into question. This is when you wonder if you'll see tomorrow.

This moment represents one of the greatest trials in the Prophet's ﷺ life and mission. Yet it also becomes, as we'll see, the turning point toward ultimate victory.

translation: And [remember] when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease said, "Allah and His Messenger did not promise us except delusion
commentary:
**وَإِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ مَّا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ إِلَّا غُرُورًا**

"And when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease were saying, 'Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ have promised us nothing but delusion.'"

The ayah distinguishes between two groups: **ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ** (the hypocrites)—those who are fully committed to disbelief internally while pretending externally—and **ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ** (those in whose hearts is disease)—those who are wavering, who are on their way to becoming full hypocrites but aren't quite there yet. Both groups, in this moment of crisis, reveal themselves.

**مَّا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ إِلَّا غُرُورًا** (Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ promised us nothing but ghurur—deception, delusion). Remember the last ayah of the previous surah warned: don't let the ultimate deceiver deceive you. Now the hypocrites are saying that Islam itself is the ultimate deception. When times got tough, they claimed they were conned into this religion, that all the promises of victory were lies.

This is the voice of those who undermine from within. When the going gets tough, they don't just lose faith—they try to take others with them, spreading their doubt and despair.

translation: And when a faction of them said, "O people of Yathrib, there is no stability for you [here], so return [home]." And a party of them asked permission of the Prophet, saying, "Indeed, our houses are unprotected," while they were not exposed. They did not intend except to flee
commentary:
**وَإِذْ قَالَت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ يَثْرِبَ لَا مُقَامَ لَكُمْ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ ۚ وَيَسْتَـْٔذِنُ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّ بُيُوتَنَا عَوْرَةٌ وَمَا هِىَ بِعَوْرَةٍ ۖ إِن يُرِيدُونَ إِلَّا فِرَارًا**

"And when a group of them said, 'O people of Yathrib, there is no place for you to stand, so go back!' And a faction of them asked permission from the Prophet ﷺ, saying, 'Indeed, our houses are exposed,' while they were not exposed. They only intended to flee."

**يَـٰٓأَهْلَ يَثْرِبَ** (O people of Yathrib)—this single word choice exposes everything. The city's name is Medina, specifically المدينة المنورة (Madinat an-Nabi, the City of the Prophet). But when the hypocrites speak, they don't call it Medina. They call it by its old name, Yathrib—the name it had before the Prophet ﷺ arrived. 

Just using the word Yathrib reveals their hypocrisy. In their hearts, they've already rejected the Prophet ﷺ. They want to return to how things were before Islam, before he came. They're calling on people's pre-Islamic emotions, trying to rally them: "Remember when this was our city, before all this started?"

**لَا مُقَامَ لَكُمْ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟** (There is no standing place for you, so go back)—you have nowhere to take your stand here. Let's retreat. Let's go back. Let's abandon this. This is the voice of Abdullah ibn Ubayy and others like him, openly trying to get people to desert.

But then there's a quieter group, more subtle: **وَيَسْتَـْٔذِنُ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ** (And a group of them ask permission from the Prophet ﷺ). They come to him respectfully, seeking permission to leave the battlefield.

**يَقُولُونَ إِنَّ بُيُوتَنَا عَوْرَةٌ** (They say, "Our houses are 'awrah"—exposed, vulnerable). They claim their homes are unprotected and they need to go back to guard them. But Allah ﷻ exposes their lie: **وَمَا هِىَ بِعَوْرَةٍ** (while they were not exposed at all). 

The Prophet ﷺ had moved all the women and children to a secure, fortified location in the center of the city for their protection. Their homes weren't in any danger. These men just wanted an excuse to get away from the potential fighting, to hide somewhere safe. **إِن يُرِيدُونَ إِلَّا فِرَارًا** (They intended nothing but to flee).

This is the corruption happening from within while the external enemy surrounds them.

translation: And if they had been entered upon from all its [surrounding] regions and fitnah had been demanded of them, they would have done it and not hesitated over it except briefly
commentary:
**وَلَوْ دُخِلَتْ عَلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَقْطَارِهَا ثُمَّ سُئِلُوا۟ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ لَـَٔاتَوْهَا وَمَا تَلَبَّثُوا۟ بِهَآ إِلَّا يَسِيرًا**

"And if the city had been entered upon them from its sides, and they had been asked to commit fitnah, they would have done it—and they would not have hesitated except very briefly."

Allah ﷻ now reveals what would have happened in a hypothetical scenario. If the enemy armies had actually breached the city (**وَلَوْ دُخِلَتْ عَلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَقْطَارِهَا**—if they had entered upon them from all sides), if 12,000 armed soldiers had flooded in and surrounded the 3,000 Muslims, if they had laid down their arms and been captured alive...

**ثُمَّ سُئِلُوا۟ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ** (And then they were asked the fitnah)—asked to renounce Islam, to abandon their faith, to declare themselves no longer Muslims. The ultimate trial.

**لَـَٔاتَوْهَا** (They would have complied immediately). These hypocrites, the ones asking permission to leave, the ones spreading defeatism—they would have caved instantly. **وَمَا تَلَبَّثُوا۟ بِهَآ إِلَّا يَسِيرًا** (And they would not have lingered over it except very little). They wouldn't have needed time to think it over, to ponder their options, to consider what they were giving up. "Yes, yes, we're not Muslims anymore! We were never really with them! We're with you now!"

This is Allah ﷻ exposing their inner reality. They're not just weak—they're ready to betray everything at the first real test. The true believers might doubt, might feel fear, but these people were already prepared to switch sides completely.

translation: And they had already promised Allah before not to turn their backs and flee. And ever is the promise to Allah [that about which one will be] questioned
commentary:
**وَلَقَدْ كَانُوا۟ عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن قَبْلُ لَا يُوَلُّونَ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ۚ وَكَانَ عَهْدُ ٱللَّهِ مَسْـُٔولًا**

"And they had certainly promised Allah ﷻ before that they would not turn their backs. And the covenant with Allah ﷻ is something that will be questioned about."

**وَلَقَدْ كَانُوا۟ عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن قَبْلُ** (And they had certainly made a covenant with Allah ﷻ before this)—these same people, in better times, had made promises. Perhaps after Badr, or after other moments of victory, they had pledged: we will stand firm, we will not flee, we will not turn our backs on this religion.

**لَا يُوَلُّونَ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ** (That they would not turn their backs)—the image is of soldiers fleeing from battle, showing their backs to the enemy. But it's also symbolic of turning away from faith, from commitment, from truth.

**وَكَانَ عَهْدُ ٱللَّهِ مَسْـُٔولًا** (And the covenant with Allah ﷻ is always something about which one will be questioned). You think you can make promises to Allah ﷻ when times are good and break them when times are hard? You will be asked about every promise you made, every pledge you gave. The covenant isn't forgotten just because the circumstances changed.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "Never will fleeing benefit you if you should flee from death or killing; and then [if you did], you would not be given enjoyment [of life] except for a little
commentary:
**قُل لَّن يَنفَعَكُمُ ٱلْفِرَارُ إِن فَرَرْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ أَوِ ٱلْقَتْلِ وَإِذًا لَّا تُمَتَّعُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا**

"Say: Fleeing will never benefit you if you flee from death or killing—and then you would not be given enjoyment except for a little while."

Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to tell them directly: **قُل لَّن يَنفَعَكُمُ ٱلْفِرَارُ** (Say: Fleeing will never benefit you). You think running away will save you? You think abandoning your post, leaving your brothers, betraying your covenant will somehow secure your life?

**إِن فَرَرْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ أَوِ ٱلْقَتْلِ** (If you flee from death or from being killed)—**ٱلْمَوْت**  is natural death, dying of illness or age; **ٱلْقَتْل**  is being killed in battle. Even if you managed to avoid both right now by running away...

**وَإِذًا لَّا تُمَتَّعُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا** (Then you would not be given enjoyment except for a brief time). You'd get a few more years, maybe. Some temporary comfort, some short-lived pleasure. And then what? Then you stand before Allah ﷻ, having broken your promises, having betrayed your brothers, having shown yourself to be a coward and a hypocrite.

The ayah dismantles the logic of self-preservation at the cost of honor and faith. Those extra years aren't worth it. That temporary safety isn't worth it. You're not escaping death—you're just postponing it and destroying yourself spiritually in the process.

translation: Say, "Who is it that can protect you from Allah if He intends for you an ill or intends for you a mercy?" And they will not find for themselves besides Allah any protector or any helper
commentary:
**قُلْ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَعْصِمُكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ سُوٓءًا أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ رَحْمَةً ۚ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا**

"Say: Who is it that can protect you from Allah ﷻ if He intends harm for you, or intends mercy for you? And they will not find for themselves besides Allah ﷻ any protector or any helper."

The rhetorical question drives the point home: **قُلْ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَعْصِمُكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ** (Say: Who is the one who can protect you from Allah ﷻ?). **يَعْصِمُ** means to shield, to hold back, to prevent. If Allah ﷻ decrees something for you, who can stop it?

**إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ سُوٓءًا** (If He intends evil/harm for you)—if He decrees that you will face difficulty, who will prevent it? You think fleeing the battlefield will save you from Allah's ﷻ decree? **أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ رَحْمَةً** (Or if He intends mercy for you)—and if He wills mercy and protection for you, who can take that away?

**وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا** (And they will not find for themselves, besides Allah ﷻ, any protective friend or any helper). They were looking for other protectors—maybe Banu Quraydha, maybe the Quraysh, maybe just running home and hiding. But none of those "protectors" can actually protect. None of those "helpers" can actually help. There is no security, no aid, no protection except with Allah ﷻ.

translation: Already Allah knows the hinderers among you and those [hypocrites] who say to their brothers, "Come to us," and do not go to battle, except for a few
commentary:
**۞ قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُعَوِّقِينَ مِنكُمْ وَٱلْقَآئِلِينَ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ هَلُمَّ إِلَيْنَا ۖ وَلَا يَأْتُونَ ٱلْبَأْسَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا**

"Allah ﷻ already knows those among you who hinder others, and those who say to their brothers, 'Come join us,' while they themselves hardly ever come to battle."

The ayah opens with certainty: **قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ** (Allah ﷻ certainly knows)—He knows exactly who they are. **ٱلْمُعَوِّقِينَ** —those who create obstacles, who obstruct others, who destroy morale. The word shares a root with **عَائِق** , which means an obstacle or barrier that prevents someone from doing something good.

These are the people actively working against the community from within. They're not just weak themselves—they're trying to make others weak. They're spreading fear, planting doubt, discouraging people from doing what needs to be done.

**وَٱلْقَآئِلِينَ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ هَلُمَّ إِلَيْنَا** (And those who say to their brothers, "Come to us!")—**هَلُمَّ** has the connotation of calling out, yelling to get someone's attention. "Hey! Come here! Come with us!" They're trying to recruit others to desert, to abandon the defense, to give up on Islam.

**وَلَا يَأْتُونَ ٱلْبَأْسَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا** (And they themselves hardly come to the battle at all). **ٱلْبَأْس** means war, conflict, the place of hardship. These people barely show up to the front lines themselves. They might make an appearance, look around, and say, "Yeah, this is bad. You should come with me if you know what's good for you." Then they disappear.

So amid the 3,000 Muslims in Medina, there were major troublemakers inside—people already ready to surrender, people actively trying to get others to desert, people spreading defeatism and fear. The external threat was massive, but the internal corruption was equally dangerous.

Yet this moment—this crisis that seemed like the end—became the turning point. The hypocrites were exposed. The believers were tested and proven true. The enemy coalition fell apart. And from this point forward, the tide turned permanently in favor of Islam. The Battle of the Trench was the last time the Quraysh would bring an army to attack the Muslims. After this, the Muslims would be on the offensive, and within a few years, Makkah itself would open to Islam without bloodshed.

Allah ﷻ preserved this entire event—the fear, the faith, the treachery, the divine intervention—as a permanent reminder: when you trust in Him completely, when you stand firm despite overwhelming odds, His help will come in ways you never imagined.

translation: Indisposed toward you. And when fear comes, you see them looking at you, their eyes revolving like one being overcome by death. But when fear departs, they lash you with sharp tongues, indisposed toward [any] good. Those have not believed, so Allah has rendered their deeds worthless, and ever is that, for Allah, easy
commentary:
أَشِحَّةً عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ ٱلْخَوْفُ رَأَيْتَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ تَدُورُ أَعْيُنُهُمْ كَٱلَّذِى يُغْشَىٰ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ فَإِذَا ذَهَبَ ٱلْخَوْفُ سَلَقُوكُم بِأَلْسِنَةٍ حِدَادٍ أَشِحَّةً عَلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ فَأَحْبَطَ ٱللَّهُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا

The word أَشِحَّةً  combines two profound character flaws into one devastating description. It merges بُخْل (bukhl), which is stinginess, with حِرْص , which is greed and excessive desire for wealth. This describes the hypocrites' contradictory relationship with the believers: when victory comes and spoils are distributed, their greed emerges and they want their share. But when it comes time to spend for the sake of Allah ﷻ or sacrifice for the community, their miserliness dominates and they withhold everything.

فَإِذَا جَآءَ ٱلْخَوْفُ (when fear arrives) – notice that Allah ﷻ uses "when" not "if." Even in the midst of crisis, there's certainty embedded in the language that this fear will eventually pass. During moments of danger, you observe these hypocrites staring at the Prophet ﷺ with accusatory eyes. Their looks seem to say, "This is your fault. You got us into this mess." 

تَدُورُ أَعْيُنُهُمْ كَٱلَّذِى يُغْشَىٰ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ – their eyes roll back like someone overtaken by death. This is theatrical, overly dramatic behavior. They make faces as if they're dying, as if the situation is unbearable and they're at death's door.

فَإِذَا ذَهَبَ ٱلْخَوْفُ (when the fear departs) – again, "when" not "if." The ayah continues with سَلَقُوكُم بِأَلْسِنَةٍ حِدَادٍ – they lash you with sharp tongues. The word سَلْق  refers to hurtful speech that crosses boundaries, words that cut and wound. These are أَلْسِنَةٍ حِدَادٍ  – tongues like metal, like scissors that slice through people's hearts with their sharpness.

We all know people with such tongues. When they criticize someone, when they insult or attack, they go all out. They don't hold back. You don't want to be their target because of how mercilessly they can cut with their words. During the battle, these hypocrites were silent or making dramatic displays of fear. But once safety returns, suddenly they become vocal critics, running their mouths, blaming the Prophet ﷺ and the believers for everything.

أَشِحَّةً عَلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ – yet even with all their criticism, they remain greedy for the خَيْر , the spoils of war. If there's an easy battle or wealth to be distributed, immediately the question becomes: "Where's our share? What do we get?" They were just complaining moments ago, but when material benefit appears, their greed resurfaces instantly.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ – these people never truly believed to begin with. Despite whatever outward displays of faith they showed, despite praying in the first row, despite attending prayers with the Prophet ﷺ, despite doing apparent good deeds – فَأَحْبَطَ ٱللَّهُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ – Allah ﷻ has nullified all their deeds. None of it counts because of their corrupt attitude. وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا – and this is easy for Allah ﷻ to do. He can cancel out all your actions because of a poisoned heart.

translation: They think the companies have not [yet] withdrawn. And if the companies should come [again], they would wish they were in the desert among the bedouins, inquiring [from afar] about your news. And if they should be among you, they would not fight except for a little
commentary:
يَحْسَبُونَ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا۟ ۖ وَإِن يَأْتِ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ يَوَدُّوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّهُم بَادُونَ فِى ٱلْأَعْرَابِ يَسْـَٔلُونَ عَنْ أَنۢبَآئِكُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ فِيكُم مَّا قَـٰتَلُوٓا۟ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

The hypocrites are so paranoid that even after the armies have retreated, يَحْسَبُونَ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا۟ – they still think the confederate forces haven't left. They're looking over their shoulders, convinced the enemy is still lurking somewhere nearby, ready to attack.  

The word الْأَحْزَابَ  refers to these confederate armies. حِزْب  is an aggressive group from a single tribe, so when multiple tribes band together, they become أَحْزَاب  – multiple confederate groups. This is why the battle is called the Battle of al-Ahzab, or the Battle of the Trench.

وَإِن يَأْتِ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ – and if these groups were to return, يَوَدُّوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّهُم بَادُونَ فِى ٱلْأَعْرَابِ – they would wish they had fled to become Bedouins in the desert. The word بَادُون  comes from البَدْو , meaning the Bedouin lifestyle. This is where the English word "Bedouin" originates. They fantasize about escaping to the desert, far from the cities where battles occur, camping with nomadic tribes where they would be safe.

يَسْـَٔلُونَ عَنْ أَنۢبَآئِكُمْ – from there, they would just ask for news about what's happening. "So, what happened to the Muslims? Did they get killed? Did they survive?" They don't want to be in the action; they want to be far away, safely tuning into "Bedouin radio" to keep track of events in Medina without any personal risk.

وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ فِيكُم مَّا قَـٰتَلُوٓا۟ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا – and had they actually been among you during the fighting, they would have fought very little. They would have been useless on the battlefield. Their contribution would have been minimal at best. This exposes the hollowness of their claims and complaints.

translation: There has certainly been for you in the Messenger of Allah an excellent pattern for anyone whose hope is in Allah and the Last Day and [who] remembers Allah often
commentary:
لَّقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِى رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِّمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ وَذَكَرَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا

Without question, in the Messenger of Allah ﷺ there has always been an excellent example for you. The word أُسْوَةٌ  is particularly meaningful here. It's related to أَسِي , which refers to someone who tends to wounds, who applies bandages, who stops bleeding and takes care of the injured. An uswah is not just a distant leader giving commands from headquarters. It's a role model characterized by compassion, mercy, concern and direct care for those he leads.

This dimension of leadership shows in the Prophet's character during al-Ahzab. He wasn't a general safely positioned away from danger, issuing orders from his quarters. He was in the thick of it himself, digging the trench alongside the Sahaba, facing the same dangers, enduring the same hunger and cold. He struggled right there with them.

Consider the absurdity of the hypocrites' accusation that the Prophet ﷺ was entering battles for worldly gain and personal wealth. What wealth? Once, Fatimah (RA), his beloved daughter who was so close to his heart, came to him showing her blistered hands from the hard labor of trying to make what little bread they had. The food had run out. She was embarrassed to ask but had no choice, so she came hoping for something – anything – to help her family. The Prophet ﷺ had nothing to give her. Instead, he taught her to say SubhanAllah thirty-three times, Alhamdulillah thirty-three times, and Allahu Akbar thirty-four times. "Take that home with you," he said.

Imagine how hard that is for a father – to have nothing material to offer your own daughter in her time of need. This is what the Messenger of Allah ﷺ lived through, even for his own children. He once told his daughter Fatimah (RA): "O daughter of Muhammad, you better watch out for Allah ﷻ because I won't be able to help you against Allah at all. I don't have the connections to help you. If you don't have your own taqwa, I cannot help you."

That messenger, who lived this way, is the أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ – the beautiful example. Every Islamic conference and convention mentions that the Prophet ﷺ is the best role model. But we need to understand where this description appears in the Quran: right in the middle of the most difficult test, at a time when people are thinking about abandoning Islam because staying Muslim might mean death. At that exact moment, Allah ﷻ says to the believers: the Messenger is the best possible example for you.

Yes, he's the best example for how to make wudu, how to enter the masjid, how to use the siwak. Those are part of his uswah. But which aspect of his example is highlighted in this surah? The macro, not just the micro. When he struggled for Islam, when he put everything on the line for the sake of Islam, he taught us that we as an ummah must be willing to do the same. We have a mission, a message to deliver to humanity. That's what truly counts.

Taking only a piece of his uswah and calling it "his Sunnah" at the expense of his greater example is an injustice. People select fragments – this aspect of his life becomes "the Sunnah," and suddenly that's what Islam amounts to, that's what his teachings amount to. If it's only a piece of the puzzle, then it remains incomplete. We must maintain the whole picture. We must maintain the entirety of his example.

لِّمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ – this example is for the one who hopes for Allah ﷻ and the Last Day. If you're hoping for riches, victories and worldly wealth, that's something else entirely. But if you want to meet Allah ﷻ, if you're hopeful for His pleasure and the Hereafter, then you won't find a better compassionate leader than the Messenger ﷺ. وَذَكَرَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا – and for someone who remembers Allah ﷻ abundantly, the Prophet ﷺ will teach them how to remember Allah properly and live that remembrance.

translation: And when the believers saw the companies, they said, "This is what Allah and His Messenger had promised us, and Allah and His Messenger spoke the truth." And it increased them only in faith and acceptance
commentary:
وَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَصَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ وَمَا زَادَهُمْ إِلَّآ إِيمَـٰنًا وَتَسْلِيمًا

When the true believers saw the multiple confederate armies approaching to annihilate them, their response was remarkable: قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ – they said, "This is what Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ promised us!"

This is astonishing. Muslims see an army coming to destroy Medina, and they say, "This is exactly what Allah ﷻ promised!" What promise are they referring to? Allah ﷻ had already told them: "Do you think you will enter Paradise without being tested? You will certainly be tested with fear, hunger, loss of wealth, loss of life and loss of fruits. Give glad tidings to those who are patient." 

The believers had been told: "Do people think they can just say 'we believe' and not be put through trials?" These tests were prophesied. So when the Sahaba saw the confederate armies surrounding them, they recognized this as the miraculous fulfillment of Allah's word. Just as they had witnessed Rome initially dominated by Persia and then making a comeback – that was a miraculous fulfillment of Quranic prophecy. Now, these armies attacking Medina represented another miraculous fulfillment.

The believers were actually overjoyed at this crisis because they could see something that non-believers simply cannot see. They possess a vision that transcends the immediate physical reality. The Quran describes those who disbelieve as having eyes but not seeing with them. While the hypocrites in the same surah complained that Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ promised them nothing but deception, the true believers looked at the exact same situation and declared it to be precisely what was promised.

وَصَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ – Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ spoke the absolute truth. وَمَا زَادَهُمْ إِلَّآ إِيمَـٰنًا وَتَسْلِيمًا – this event did nothing except increase them in faith and increase them in submission. The word تَسْلِيمًا  suggests continuous, ongoing submission. They became even more submissive to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ through this trial.

translation: Among the believers are men true to what they promised Allah. Among them is he who has fulfilled his vow [to the death], and among them is he who awaits [his chance]. And they did not alter [the terms of their commitment] by any alteration
commentary:
مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا۟ مَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّن قَضَىٰ نَحْبَهُۥ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنتَظِرُ ۖ وَمَا بَدَّلُوا۟ تَبْدِيلًا

Among the believers are real men – رِجَالٌ , not just males, but men of substance and character. صَدَقُوا۟ مَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ – they have been true to their covenant with Allah ﷻ. They fulfilled their promise to Him.

The word نَحْب  comes from نَذْر , which is a pledge or vow. It's when you dedicate something and make it mandatory upon yourself. Like saying, "If I get into this college, I'll fast for three days" – that's a nadhr. The Sahaba essentially said: "Ya Allah, give me Jannah. I offer You my life in exchange." This was their transaction with Allah ﷻ. Indeed, Allah ﷻ says elsewhere: "Allah has purchased from the believers their lives and their wealth in exchange for Paradise."

فَمِنْهُم مَّن قَضَىٰ نَحْبَهُۥ – among them are those who have already completed their pledge, who have already made their payment. They've already given their lives and gone to Jannah. وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنتَظِرُ – and among them are those who are waiting to make their payment. They're waiting, even begging Allah ﷻ: "Don't let my days go by in waste. Don't let my time pass in mere entertainment. Make me among those who make their payment to You. Let me serve Your religion. Let me do something meaningful for You."

وَمَا بَدَّلُوا۟ تَبْدِيلًا – they would never change this priority for anything in the world. There's nothing more valuable to them than this commitment. They wouldn't replace this enthusiasm to serve the religion with any worldly alternative.

translation: That Allah may reward the truthful for their truth and punish the hypocrites if He wills or accept their repentance. Indeed, Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
لِّيَجْزِىَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ بِصِدْقِهِمْ وَيُعَذِّبَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ إِن شَآءَ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

The purpose is so that Allah ﷻ may reward the truthful on account of their truthfulness. وَيُعَذِّبَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ إِن شَآءَ – and He may punish the hypocrites if He wills. Notice: "if He wills." It may be that the hypocrites get punished, or it may be that they don't. This rests entirely in Allah's hands.

This phrasing means the hypocrite doesn't get to feel safe, secure in avoiding consequences. At the same time, the Muslim doesn't get to assume that hypocrites will definitely be destroyed. We don't have that authority to declare such verdicts. Allah ﷻ reserves the right – إِن شَآءَ.

أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ – or, if they get their act together, He might turn back to them and give them a second chance. Because that opportunity for repentance is coming soon. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا – indeed, Allah ﷻ has always been Extremely Forgiving, Continuously Merciful. The doors of His mercy remain open even for those who demonstrated hypocrisy during this most difficult trial.

translation: And Allah repelled those who disbelieved, in their rage, not having obtained any good. And sufficient was Allah for the believers in battle, and ever is Allah Powerful and Exalted in Might
commentary:
وَرَدَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِغَيْظِهِمْ لَمْ يَنَالُوا۟ خَيْرًا ۚ وَكَفَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلْقِتَالَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ قَوِيًّا عَزِيزًا

Allah ﷻ turned back those who disbelieved, sending them away with their rage and frustration. They came with anger and the intent to unleash it upon the Muslims, but they left with that same rage – unspent, unfulfilled, turning inward upon themselves.

لَمْ يَنَالُوا۟ خَيْرًا – they obtained no good whatsoever. Not a single spoil of war. They spent tremendous amounts of money to assemble this coalition. They expended enormous political capital – not just wealth, but the political will required to bring that many tribes together. And nothing good came of it. It was a complete waste.

وَكَفَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلْقِتَالَ – and Allah ﷻ Himself was sufficient for the believers in the fighting. Allah ﷻ did much of the fighting for them. They didn't have to do all the fighting; Allah ﷻ fought for them. وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ قَوِيًّا عَزِيزًا – He has always been Ultimately Powerful, the Final Authority.

translation: And He brought down those who supported them among the People of the Scripture from their fortresses and cast terror into their hearts [so that] a party you killed, and you took captive a party
commentary:
وَأَنزَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَـٰهَرُوهُم مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن صَيَاصِيهِمْ وَقَذَفَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلرُّعْبَ فَرِيقًا تَقْتُلُونَ وَتَأْسِرُونَ فَرِيقًا

When the Makkan armies retreated, the Prophet ﷺ turned his attention to Banu Qurayzah, the Jewish tribe that had betrayed the covenant during the siege. A remarkable incident occurred: the Prophet ﷺ was removing his armor when Jibreel (AS) came to him and asked, "What are you doing?" He informed the Prophet ﷺ that while the angel armies had dealt with the external threat, there was still Banu Qurayzah to address.

The Prophet ﷺ instructed the Sahaba: "None of you should pray Asr until you reach Banu Qurayzah." The Companions rushed toward the Jewish fortresses. Some arrived in time and prayed Asr at its proper time. Others arrived after the time had passed and reasoned that the Prophet ﷺ had said not to pray until reaching Qurayzah, so they prayed after arrival despite the time having elapsed. This famous difference of opinion among scholars originates from that moment – both groups understood the Prophet's instruction sincerely, arriving at different applications.

Allah ﷻ says: وَأَنزَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَـٰهَرُوهُم مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن صَيَاصِيهِمْ – He brought down those who had supported the enemy from among the People of the Book, bringing them down from their fortresses.

The word صَيَاصِي  comes from صِيصَة, referring to the horns of a bull. A fortress described as sisa is one bristling with defensive and offensive capabilities – places to shoot arrows from, positions to pour boiling tar, ramparts with weapons protruding like a bull's horns ready to attack. Banu Qurayzah possessed such fortified castles designed to protect and strike at attackers. Yet Allah ﷻ brought them down from these supposedly impregnable positions.

وَقَذَفَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلرُّعْبَ – He cast into their hearts رُعْب , which is extreme terror, a fear so overwhelming that you cannot think straight, that paralyzes you and makes you utterly timid. These people, despite their fortifications, simply gave up. They lost all will to resist.

The verdict on Banu Qurayzah came from Sa'd ibn Mu'adh (RA), who had been from their tribe before embracing Islam. They requested him as their judge, thinking he would be lenient. They didn't realize he would judge according to their own scripture. Sa'd (RA) recited the verdict from the Torah itself: in cases of treachery during war, all males capable of fighting are to be executed, and everyone else taken as prisoners. This was their own law, which they had requested, not knowing what it actually contained. This became one of the most severe punishments executed after the Battle of the Trench.

فَرِيقًا تَقْتُلُونَ وَتَأْسِرُونَ فَرِيقًا – a group you killed and a group you took as prisoners.

translation: And He caused you to inherit their land and their homes and their properties and a land which you have not trodden. And ever is Allah, over all things, competent
commentary:
وَأَوْرَثَكُمْ أَرْضَهُمْ وَدِيَـٰرَهُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلَهُمْ وَأَرْضًا لَّمْ تَطَـُٔوهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرًا

Allah ﷻ gave the believers an inheritance – their land, their homes, their wealth. The lands of Banu Qurayzah became Muslim territory. Then comes something extraordinary: وَأَرْضًا لَّمْ تَطَـُٔوهَا – and land that you have not even set foot upon yet.

Allah ﷻ is promising even more conquests, lands the Muslims haven't even thought about, territories they haven't imagined reaching. What lands could these be?

To understand this prophecy's fulfillment, consider what happened to Makkah's reputation after al-Ahzab. The Quraysh had never looked worse. Three times they tried to eliminate the Muslims. The first time at Badr, they failed. The second time at Uhud, they failed. The third time at al-Ahzab, they convinced multiple tribes to join them, assembled the largest coalition ever seen, and still failed spectacularly.

But here's the crucial point: all the other tribes who participated weren't inherently passionate about fighting Muslims. They only came because the Quraysh convinced them. When everyone left as failures, those tribes looked at the Quraysh and thought: "You couldn't even handle this yourselves. You needed our help. You wasted our time, our resources, our money on these Muslims. Why would we ever help you again?"

The Quraysh used to command universal respect throughout Arabia. Because of this incident, their reputation collapsed completely. This is critical to understanding what follows.

The Battle of al-Ahzab occurred in the fifth year after the Hijrah. Just one year later, in the sixth year, the Prophet ﷺ made an astounding announcement: "Let's go perform Hajj." Think about this timeline. A year ago, Medina was surrounded, Muslims were facing potential genocide. Not even a full year has passed, and the Prophet ﷺ says, "Let's go to Makkah for Hajj. It's voluntary – whoever wants to come can join."

Would they take weapons? You don't take shields and armor to Hajj. They would take their swords – the "Arab passport" – and daggers for slaughtering animals, but not full military equipment. The hypocrites in Medina were thrilled: "He's lost his mind. We just barely survived. Now he wants to walk into Makkah? He's going to get himself killed, and those fanatical believers are going with him. Good riddance. They won't be coming back."

But the Prophet ﷺ wanted to test exactly how weakened the Quraysh had become. When the Quraysh learned that the Muslims were approaching for Hajj, they sent Khalid ibn Walid (not yet Muslim) with 200 cavalry to intercept them in the desert. Why the desert? Because they couldn't attack pilgrims near the Kaaba. Whatever little respect the Quraysh retained rested on their guardianship of the Kaaba, and universal Arab custom absolutely forbade attacking anyone who came to perform pilgrimage. If they violated that sanctity, they would become targets for all of Arabia.

The Prophet's scouts informed him of Khalid's position, so the Muslims took a detour and stopped at Hudaybiyyah. This location was significant – it was public territory where pilgrims were untouchable. By positioning themselves there, the Muslims became completely protected by Arabian custom. Any attack now would be witnessed by all of Arabia.

This strategic move weakened the Quraysh like never before. The mere fact that they were willing to negotiate – the famous Treaty of Hudaybiyyah – announced to all of Arabia that the Quraysh had become the weaker party. Who negotiates? The loser tries to negotiate with the winner. The winner simply says, "I'm going to destroy you." The hypocrites wanted to negotiate because they were losers. Now the Quraysh themselves were officially negotiating.

Yes, the treaty seemed to have harsh terms against the Muslims. But the political significance of the Quraysh offering to negotiate at all changed everything. Every tribe in Arabia understood: these Muslims are remarkably strong. They got the mighty Quraysh to come to the negotiating table. Last year, the Quraysh tried to annihilate them in Medina. This year, the Muslims showed up at Makkah's doorstep and scared them so much they agreed to negotiate. Suddenly, nobody respected the Quraysh anymore.

After the ceasefire was signed, the Prophet ﷺ went to tribes throughout the region with an offer: "There's now a ceasefire between us and Makkah. You have options: sign a treaty to remain neutral, let us know if you're with the Quraysh, or you can join us as allies."

The results were transformative. Many tribes declared neutrality – "We want nothing to do with this conflict. Keep us out of it. We'll be like Switzerland." But a huge number became allies with the Muslims. So tribes either allied with Muslims or remained neutral. Almost none sided with the Quraysh anymore. The political dominance that Makkah had enjoyed – that universal respect from all the surrounding tribes – vanished completely. All because of al-Ahzab.

The Prophet ﷺ had been dealing with ongoing threats in Medina but couldn't fully address them while the Makkan danger loomed. Now, with the ceasefire in place, he could turn attention to Khaybar. The Jewish tribes exiled from Medina for their treachery had established major military encampments in Khaybar, from where they launched attacks against Muslims. With the ceasefire preventing the Quraysh from aiding them, the time came to address Khaybar.

This is the fulfillment of وَأَرْضًا لَّمْ تَطَـُٔوهَا – land you haven't stepped foot upon. The vast territories of Khaybar – enormous expanses of gardens, farmland, greenery, military resources – came under Muslim control. Acres upon acres of land, resources they had never seen before. And Allah ﷻ didn't even describe it as a difficult fight. The word used suggests trampling, crushing – it wouldn't even be a real battle. That's exactly what happened. Khaybar fell without the prolonged struggle one might have expected.

Consider the Prophet's foresight ﷺ. As the confederate armies were leaving after al-Ahzab in the fifth year, he told the Muslims: "Quraysh will not do battle with you after this day, but you will battle them. They will not come after you. After this day, it is over." He called the victory before anyone else could see it.

Allah ﷻ put the believers through this incredible test, and it changed the entire course of the Prophet's mission. World events hinged on Medina surviving this siege. It's a truly remarkable turning point in history.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرًا – Allah ﷻ has always been completely capable over all things. His power orchestrated events that transformed not just a city or a region, but the trajectory of human civilization itself.

What's profound about Surah al-Ahzab is that despite discussing this monumental military event, it's not named after the battle itself. The surah is still called "al-Ahzab" (The Confederates), and it frames this massive conflict within discussions of domestic and social matters. Don't underestimate the significance of family life and social ethics. A nation isn't only about military might and conquest. A nation is built on families, on social morality, on domestic stability. 

Islam refuses to let us lose sight of either dimension. You must maintain both the macro view and the micro view simultaneously. The surah moves seamlessly from military matters to social issues to family concerns. This is the wisdom of Allah ﷻ – keeping all these elements together in one surah rather than separating them into different chapters. This teaches us to maintain balance, to never become so consumed by one aspect that we neglect the others.

translation: O Prophet, say to your wives, "If you should desire the worldly life and its adornment, then come, I will provide for you and give you a gracious release
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ قُل لِّأَزْوَٰجِكَ إِن كُنتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا فَتَعَالَيْنَ أُمَتِّعْكُنَّ وَأُسَرِّحْكُنَّ سَرَاحًا جَمِيلًا

The opening address carries profound weight. Allah ﷻ doesn't use the word رسول ( messenger) here, but rather نبي (prophet). The word نبي comes from نبأ, which means to deliver significant news - not something trivial, but momentous revelation. When Allah ﷻ uses this particular title, it signals that major policy and weighty guidance are about to be delivered.

This ayah arrives at a crucial moment in Islamic history. The Battle of Al-Ahzab had just concluded. The Confederate armies had withdrawn, leaving behind substantial spoils of war. The Muslims had also achieved an easy victory at Banu Quraydhah, acquiring military supplies and resources. Medina had transformed virtually overnight from complete economic drainage - where there wasn't even enough food in the city - to a state of surplus and economic boom.

The mothers of the believers noticed that while prosperity spread throughout Medina, their household budgets remained unchanged. We know from numerous narrations that in the Prophet's ﷺ household, the stove rarely lit more than once a day. They were living well below even hand-to-mouth subsistence. So they respectfully approached the Prophet ﷺ with a modest request: since times were better for the Muslims generally, perhaps their household budgets could increase slightly. They weren't seeking jewelry or luxury shopping - just enough to ease their daily struggles with groceries and basic necessities.

This request, though seemingly reasonable, touched on something fundamental. The standards Allah ﷻ holds for His Prophet ﷺ cannot be compared to the standards for other believers. For most Muslims, if we avoid the haram and fulfill our obligatory duties with some additional good deeds, we're on solid ground. But the Messenger ﷺ is held to the constant standard of ihsan - excellence - and even the slightest decrease from that level is considered a decline. Consider this: fasting is optional for us outside Ramadan, and if we manage it, it's praiseworthy. For the Prophet ﷺ, it was mandatory. Acts that are supererogatory for us were obligations for him.

Think of it like different levels of responsibility. As responsible citizens, we should pick up garbage when we see it on the street, but we're not legally obligated to do so. The Sanitation Department, however, is charged with that responsibility - it's not just nice of them to collect garbage, they must. Similarly, the Prophet ﷺ is charged with certain responsibilities at a level of obligation that differs from what's expected of us.

When the mothers of the believers made their request, the Prophet ﷺ became so upset that he made i'la - a separation from his wives. This doesn't mean divorce; rather, it means withdrawing from spending time with them, not visiting their quarters. For an entire month, he didn't speak to any of them. Umar (RA) was terrified that his daughter Hafsah (RA), who was married to the Prophet ﷺ, might be divorced. Aisha (RA) was similarly frightened about what might happen.

Then Allah ﷻ revealed this passage with its stern language. The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to tell his wives: "If you have been wanting worldly life and its beauties..." They weren't asking for mansions or jewelry - just groceries. But their standards are so elevated that they must accept a life of extreme sacrifice. They're with the Messenger ﷺ who gives everything for this mission. They must emulate that level of commitment.

"If you want those things, fine. Come forward, and I will provide for you generously. I will give you luxury and let you go gracefully." The word أُسَرِّحْكُنَّ  means "I will let you go" - indicating divorce. When cattle are released to graze freely, that's sarah. "I'll divorce you if worldly life is what you seek. Whatever spoils came, I'll ensure you have more than you asked for. But if you receive that, you cannot remain in my household."

Why this harsh response? The Prophet ﷺ doesn't deny dunya because he hates it. He himself stated that two things were made beloved to him in this world: good perfume and women. But when you're on a critical mission - and he absolutely is - everything else becomes secondary. If there's only enough food for lunch, dinner is secondary. The mission comes first. Until that mission is complete, there can be no relaxation, no expansion of worldly assets, no building of personal empires.

The Prophet ﷺ exists in a constant state of emergency. He cannot entertain the mentality of "let's have a few more groceries." If the leadership of this movement shows even the slightest laxity, it will multiply exponentially down through the ranks. No one will sacrifice like the Prophet ﷺ does, but they will sacrifice some fraction of what he sacrifices. If that original sacrifice - the Prophet's ﷺ - is compromised even one percent, it will trickle down to a twenty percent compromise for the next follower. The highest concentration of sacrifice, the purest sacrifice, must be maintained at the top.

The mothers of the believers have taken on something extraordinarily heavy. People justify their lifestyle choices by looking at those more religious or knowledgeable than themselves. When someone sees that the imam has a big screen TV, they think, "Well then, we can get one too." The fanciest wedding they attend becomes their benchmark. Followers justify their behavior based on leadership, and no one would look to leadership more than to the Prophet ﷺ and his family.

There's another dimension: the hypocrites were constantly seeking scandals. They had already attempted to create one involving Aisha (RA) that led to the revelation of Surah An-Nur. They were looking for scandals involving any of the Prophet's ﷺ wives. These hypocrites knew how to spin any situation, how to scandalize the most innocent matters. If they saw even a slight increase in the Prophet's ﷺ household groceries, they would gossip: "Look, he's feeding his family well now! Times are good for the Prophet, aren't they?" They possessed sharp tongues ready to attack with offensive language at any opportunity for gain, as we learned earlier in this very surah.

translation: But if you should desire Allah and His Messenger and the home of the Hereafter - then indeed, Allah has prepared for the doers of good among you a great reward
commentary:
وَإِن كُنتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْمُحْسِنَـٰتِ مِنكُنَّ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا

But if you desire Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ and the home of the Hereafter, then Allah ﷻ has prepared tremendous compensation for those among you who do ihsan.

The choice is presented clearly. Aisha (RA) was the first to respond, declaring immediately that she chose Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ. All the other mothers of the believers followed her lead, making the same choice.

Notice the conditional nature of Allah's ﷻ promise: just because they made this choice doesn't mean Allah ﷻ automatically guarantees them tremendous reward. They must reach the level of المُحْسِنَات - those who excel, those who achieve ihsan. For us, ihsan means consistency in good deeds. But by Allah's ﷻ standards, for those in the household of prophethood, the bar for ihsan is set even higher. They are muhsinat by our standards constantly, but by Allah's ﷻ standards, they must push themselves to yet another level.

The message is clear: don't even think about what you'll receive in this world if you truly appreciate what Allah ﷻ has prepared for you in the next.

translation: O wives of the Prophet, whoever of you should commit a clear immorality - for her the punishment would be doubled two fold, and ever is that, for Allah, easy
commentary:
يَـٰنِسَآءَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ مَن يَأْتِ مِنكُنَّ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ يُضَـٰعَفْ لَهَا ٱلْعَذَابُ ضِعْفَيْنِ ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا

Then comes an address unlike any other in the Quran. Allah ﷻ addresses the wives of the Prophet ﷺ directly: يَـٰنِسَآءَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ (O women of the Prophet). He doesn't call other believers "O followers of the Prophet" or use similar phrasing for anyone else. But He addresses the mothers of the believers directly and by name.

The surah began by addressing the Prophet ﷺ directly. Now we see direct address to his wives. The timing is deliberate. Allah ﷻ warns first with the most serious consequence: if any among them were to commit a clear, open indecency (فَاحِشَة), her punishment would be doubled.

We cannot even imagine such a thing from the mothers of the believers - may Allah ﷻ be pleased with them all. So why mention it? Because Islam teaches by establishing the most serious warning first. When people hear this severe consequence, their immediate reaction is: "If that's the punishment for something we would never do, we need to be extremely careful about everything else." This creates heightened awareness and vigilance.

The doubling of punishment has profound reasoning. Some scholars explain it's doubled because the mothers of the believers have more access to guidance than any other women. Every believing woman learns from Quran and Sunnah, but no believing woman has more direct access to Quran and more immediate access to the living Sunnah than the wives of the Prophet ﷺ. They witness revelation coming down. They see the Prophet ﷺ implementing guidance in his daily life. They have the opportunity to ask questions directly. With such unprecedented access to guidance comes proportionate responsibility. To whom much is given, much is required.

Others note that because they serve as role models, any sin wouldn't be just their personal transgression - it would be a failure to maintain the standard of uswah (exemplary conduct). The sin becomes magnified because of its impact on the entire community of believers who look to them for guidance.

But don't think connections to the Prophet ﷺ create immunity or special treatment: وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا - "And that is easy for Allah." Being married to the Prophet ﷺ doesn't make accountability difficult for Allah ﷻ to carry out. He will hold them accountable with perfect justice, and that is utterly easy for Him.

translation: And whoever of you devoutly obeys Allah and His Messenger and does righteousness - We will give her her reward twice; and We have prepared for her a noble provision
commentary:
۞ وَمَن يَقْنُتْ مِنكُنَّ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَتَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا نُّؤْتِهَآ أَجْرَهَا مَرَّتَيْنِ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لَهَا رِزْقًا كَرِيمًا

The tone shifts. After establishing the serious consequences, the orientation begins anew. Now comes the honor. Whoever among them shows complete qunoot - readiness, willingness, and eagerness to obey Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ - and acts righteously will receive her reward twice.

The qunoot shown to the Prophet ﷺ cannot be compared to the obedience a wife shows to any other husband. This is categorically different. The wife of Umar (RA) or the wife of Abu Bakr (RA) - their relationship with their husbands doesn't carry the same weight as the relationship between the mothers of the believers and the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Why? Because they're not just dealing with a husband; they're dealing with the Messenger of Allah ﷻ.

This distinction is crucial because husbands cannot quote these ayat to demand from their wives what the Prophet ﷺ could rightfully expect from his. Yes, there is a degree of respectful obedience a wife should show her husband in Islam. But it cannot be equated with what these ayat demand of the mothers of the believers. What the Prophet ﷺ asks of them - the level of sacrifice, the difficulty of the challenges - far exceeds what any other believing woman must undertake for her husband.

When they demonstrate this qunoot and perform righteous deeds, they receive double compensation: once for being believers, and again for going beyond, for being role models and examples of uswah for all women. And Allah ﷻ has prepared for them رِزْقًا كَرِيمًا - noble, gracious, dignified provision.

translation: O wives of the Prophet, you are not like anyone among women. If you fear Allah, then do not be soft in speech [to men], lest he in whose heart is disease should covet, but speak with appropriate speech
commentary:
يَـٰنِسَآءَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ لَسْتُنَّ كَأَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۚ إِنِ ٱتَّقَيْتُنَّ فَلَا تَخْضَعْنَ بِٱلْقَوْلِ فَيَطْمَعَ ٱلَّذِى فِى قَلْبِهِۦ مَرَضٌ وَقُلْنَ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا

Once again: يَـٰنِسَآءَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ (O women of the Prophet). The first time this address appeared, it concerned the possibility of evil deeds. Now that the foundational orientation is complete, the honor truly begins.

You are not like any other women. Just as the Prophet ﷺ cannot be compared to anyone else - وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ (know well that among you is the Messenger of Allah) - you as the role model household cannot be compared to any other women.
