You stand on a higher moral platform because from you, the highest standards will be learned and implemented.

The conditional follows: إِنِ ٱتَّقَيْتُنَّ (If you maintain taqwa). Then comes specific, practical guidance: فَلَا تَخْضَعْنَ بِٱلْقَوْلِ (do not be soft in speech).

The word تَخْضَعْنَ means to humble yourself, to soften, to make your voice gentle and pleasant. Allah ﷻ is acknowledging a natural reality: women generally have softer, more pleasant voices than men. This isn't a criticism - it's recognition of how Allah ﷻ created them. Men are naturally attracted to women's voices. When they hear a woman speak, it sounds pleasant to them, and they may seek to prolong the conversation.

This isn't unique to Muslims - it's human nature. This is why customer service roles predominantly employ women. Sales calls made by women get hung up on less frequently than those made by men. The voice of a woman is attractive in a way that can be exploited or can become a fitna.

The Prophet's ﷺ wives are told: there are people in Medina looking to create scandals involving you, people with diseases in their hearts. Even if you simply say "Wa alaikum salaam" in a soft, gentle voice, something will stir inside them. They'll think, "The way she said salaam... something about it..." Their imaginations will begin working.

Young women today need to understand this reality. When you're at an Islamic convention and a young man approaches, and you respond pleasantly - "Sure, you can sit here" - that young man may spend the entire day replaying that moment, convinced of things that don't exist. You must adopt a firmer tone. You cannot be naive about the effect of your voice and manner on men whose hormones and imaginations are easily triggered.

The mothers of the believers would have trained and untrained companions coming to learn from them - seasoned believers and new Muslims seeking knowledge. Even after the Prophet's ﷺ passing, people would come to learn from them. If they heard a soft, gentle voice, the fitna wouldn't diminish just because the source is a mother of the believers. For some, it would intensify precisely because it's a mother of the believers - and what greater coup for those seeking to destroy the Prophet's ﷺ household?

Therefore, the mothers of the believers, despite being teachers, must adopt a stern tone. Don't soften your speech. Your nature is to be kind - be kind to other women. But when men come asking questions, maintain a firm voice so they don't get ideas, so the one with disease in his heart doesn't start having wandering thoughts and long hopes.

وَقُلْنَ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا (And speak decent words). This means: don't talk about just anything. Don't make small talk. Only discuss what needs to be discussed. Don't engage in conversation for conversation's sake.

I need to share my personal conviction here, though I'm neither a faqih nor a mufti. Some scholars may take an easier stance than what I've adopted for my family, while others may take a tougher stance. But in my household - and alhamdulillah, my wife shares this conviction strongly - we maintain a rule: I don't make small talk with sisters. When couples visit, I don't engage the wife in extended conversation: "How are your studies going? How's your family?" I let my wife ask those questions. I can say salaam and move on. Similarly, I don't want other men engaging my wife in extended conversations. If they do, they'll receive a look that communicates clearly: talk to me instead.

Some might consider this rude. "We're supposed to have couples dinners, socialize together, laugh and chat." But where's the firm voice in that setting? Where's the boundary? Are people laughing and joking together freely? "That's so funny!" - back and forth banter? You think disease of the heart doesn't exist in such environments? The mothers of the believers, regarding whom no evil could even be imagined, are told to take this tough stance. What are we supposed to think - that we're immune? That these rules were only for them because they had higher standards, but we can freely chitchat at parties and gatherings?

Eid parties are coming. There are two kinds: Eid gatherings that fortify your iman and seal the value of all your Ramadan worship, and Eid parties that multiply all of Ramadan by zero. Both exist.

قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا - speak decent, appropriate things. "Brother, could you call my husband? We need to go." "Okay, fine." That's sufficient. "Could you call my husband?" "Okay, hello." Done. Take a firm voice.

translation: And abide in your houses and do not display yourselves as [was] the display of the former times of ignorance. And establish prayer and give zakah and obey Allah and His Messenger. Allah intends only to remove from you the impurity [of sin], O people of the [Prophet's] household, and to purify you with [extensive] purification
commentary:
وَقَرْنَ فِى بُيُوتِكُنَّ وَلَا تَبَرَّجْنَ تَبَرُّجَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ ۖ وَأَقِمْنَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتِينَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِعْنَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنكُمُ ٱلرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيرًا

The word وَقَرْنَ  requires linguistic analysis to appreciate its full meaning. Scholars derive it from several possible roots, each adding layers of significance.

Some trace it to قَرَار - to be settled somewhere and comfortable there. It's related to the idea of stopping movement, of finding rest. The pattern is قَرَّ , but one of the letters is removed, making it softer: قَرْن . The meaning: find comfort at home. Be relaxed at home. Be settled at home.

Others connect it to وَقَار  - dignity, poise, composure. This reading emphasizes: have dignity in being home. Don't consider it beneath you to be home. "Why do I have to stay home?" shouldn't be your question. There's honor in this. That's the essence of waqar.

The word also relates to رَزَانَة  - according to classical Arabic, a woman of razanah possesses firmness, dignity, and protects herself. She maintains her place and station with grace.

All these meanings converge in this command: commit yourselves primarily to your homes. The mothers of the believers are told their primary sphere is the home. They should only leave for genuine necessity. Leaving with family is one matter; going out alone without real need is another. If there's no milk and the husband is at work, go to the store. But strolling through the mall for entertainment? No - remain at home unless absolutely necessary.

وَلَا تَبَرَّجْنَ (And do not make tabarruj). Tabarruj literally means displaying yourself, beautifying and decking yourself out for public view. It means dressing provocatively, dousing yourself in so much makeup that your natural features disappear under layers of color, wearing glittery clothes and heavy jewelry so that when you walk, people hear you before they see you - the sound of chains and bangles announcing your presence.

Some aspects of a woman's appearance simply cannot be hidden - her height, her build, her basic form even in loose clothing. But anything beyond what cannot be hidden should be concealed. Don't engage in the tabarruj of الْجَاهِلِيَّةِ الْأُولَىٰ (the first jahiliyyah) - ancient ignorance.

The phrase الْأُولَىٰ (the first) implies you've left that era far behind. Don't return to it. Historically, before Islam, the word tabarruj was used for slave women known to be questionable in character - not for decent women. Allah ﷻ is declaring: now that Islam has freed you and made you women of dignity and decency, you cannot dress like those women whose character was questionable. That way of dressing indicates a certain lifestyle and reputation. You're beyond that now.

Notice this ayah combines two instructions: stay at home when possible, and when you do go out, don't beautify yourself excessively. What does this imply? Beautify yourselves at home. The ayah doesn't mean look unkempt all the time. Between the lines, it's actually encouraging women to beautify themselves in their homes - and we'll explore the significance of that in our adult discussion session.

وَأَقِمْنَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتِينَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ (And establish prayer and give zakah). The mothers of the believers receive this command directly. Your salah is your own obligation. Your zakah is your own obligation. Your religion is yours personally. It's not as if the head of household covers everything for you. You have your own duties.

Notice they're commanded to give their own zakah. I derive from this that men and women, even in the same household, calculate zakah differently. Their wealth may be separate, their zakah obligations distinct, because the command came to them separately.

وَأَطِعْنَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ (Continue to obey Allah and His Messenger). The emphasis on continuity - keep obeying, maintain obedience.

إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنكُمُ ٱلرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ (Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity, O people of the household). This is the great address: أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ. The use of يَا (ya) makes it a vocative - a direct, intimate call. "O family, O household of the Prophet." The form without يَا turns it into emphasis: "family of the house."

Ar-rijs - filth, impurity - includes both physical and moral corruption, but particularly moral impurity and immorality. It also implies scandal. Allah ﷻ doesn't want you involved in scandal, because if you dress even slightly provocatively and someone with filth in his heart sees you and starts gossiping, scandal about the mothers of the believers would devastate Islam. The house of the Messenger ﷺ must be fortified. Just as Medina is now physically secure, the Prophet's ﷺ household must be made spiritually and socially secure.

وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيرًا (And to purify you with thorough purification). The emphasis - تَطْهِيرًا  after يُطَهِّرَكُمْ - indicates complete, absolute purification. Allah ﷻ wants to cleanse you beyond all cleansing, purify you to a degree beyond what's required of anyone else.

translation: And remember what is recited in your houses of the verses of Allah and wisdom. Indeed, Allah is ever Subtle and Acquainted [with all things]
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرْنَ مَا يُتْلَىٰ فِى بُيُوتِكُنَّ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْحِكْمَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ لَطِيفًا خَبِيرًا

Make mention of, remember, reflect upon what is recited in your homes from the ayat of Allah ﷻ and al-hikmah (wisdom).

Revelation descends in your homes. If anyone should want to stay home, it should be the mothers of the believers, because Quran comes down inside the house. The angel Jibreel (AS) enters the home. This is one of the unique honors of Aisha (RA) that the Prophet ﷺ himself described: she's the only one under whose blanket revelation came. The Prophet ﷺ was lying down, they were sharing a blanket, and angel Jibreel (AS) came with revelation. The angel doesn't knock and wait for permission - he comes from the Master to the slave on divine command, and the Prophet ﷺ is himself a slave who must receive.

There's a famous narration about the last ayat of Surah Ali Imran being revealed while the Prophet ﷺ was lying with Aisha (RA). When revelation began coming, he turned to her and asked permission to engage in worship. She gave permission, and he began making lengthy prostrations as those ayat were revealed.

This narration becomes particularly relevant now because after the Prophet's ﷺ passing, the companions (RA) missed the Messenger ﷺ so intensely that they came to the mothers of the believers to learn. They would come and, from behind hijab, ask the mothers of the believers to share memories and teachings. Why from behind hijab? These are the mothers of the believers - why would there be concern? We're about to learn why in the coming ayat.

Aisha (RA) would tell them: everything about him was unique. But let me share something specific - and then she would relate these precious memories.

Al-hikmah here refers to the Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ - the way he applied the ayat of Allah ﷻ in practice.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ لَطِيفًا خَبِيرًا (Indeed, Allah has always been Latif, fully aware). Latif - subtle, gentle, kind in ways that penetrate to the finest details. Khabir - thoroughly, completely, intimately aware of everything.

This was the grand address to the believing women, specifically the mothers of the believers, establishing them as role models.

translation: Indeed, the Muslim men and Muslim women, the believing men and believing women, the obedient men and obedient women, the truthful men and truthful women, the patient men and patient women, the humble men and humble women, the charitable men and charitable women, the fasting men and fasting women, the men who guard their private parts and the women who do so, and the men who remember Allah often and the women who do so - for them Allah has prepared forgiveness and a great reward
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ وَٱلْمُسْلِمَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ وَٱلْصَّـٰدِقَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرَٰتِ وَٱلْخَـٰشِعِينَ وَٱلْخَـٰشِعَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰٓئِمِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰٓئِمَـٰتِ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظِينَ فُرُوجَهُمْ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظَـٰتِ وَٱلذَّٰكِرِينَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱلذَّٰكِرَٰتِ أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا

This ayah is remarkably similar in theme and placement to ayah 35 of Surah An-Nur. That ayah described the light of iman inside a believer - نُورًا عَلَىٰ نُورٍ (light upon light). This ayah, also numbered 35, describes what that internal iman looks like externally - the characteristics and qualities that light produces in a person's behavior and life.

Though we know that in Arabic, saying الْمُسْلِمِينَ includes both men and women grammatically, this surah is particular in offering guidance especially relevant to women. Women should pay extra attention to this surah's teachings, as already highlighted in these central ayat.

The ayah lists pairs - men and women - possessing these qualities:

The Muslim men and Muslim women - those who have submitted themselves completely to Allah ﷻ. The believing men and believing women - those with absolute conviction and faith, not just outward submission but internal certainty.

The قَانِتِين and قَانِتَات - those ready, willing, and even enthusiastic to obey. There's eagerness in their obedience, not reluctance.

The truthful men and truthful women - الصَّادِقِين encompasses both sidq (truthfulness) and tasdiq (confirmation). They're people who learn truth and live by it. They confirm with their lives what they affirm with their tongues.

The patient and persevering men and women - الصَّابِرِين. This isn't passive patience but active perseverance through difficulty.

The خَاشِعِين and خَاشِعَات - those in awe of Allah ﷻ, those overwhelmed with fear of disappointing Him, terrified of disobeying Him.

Notice how the ayah progresses, moving closer to home, touching on more personal matters. The next quality: الْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقَـٰتِ - those who give in charity, men and women.

Why is this mentioned after describing the wives of the Prophet ﷺ who have such high standards? They were told not to ask for more. But here's the reality: they shouldn't be concerned with getting - they should focus on giving. These standards are miles above us, yet we're being shown the path.

الصَّائِمِينَ وَٱلصَّائِمَـٰتِ - those who fast, men and women. Sadaqah represents the figurative giving of yourself - giving from the wealth that represents part of your life's energy. Fasting is literally giving up part of your body's needs and energy.

Then comes the most directly relevant connection to the previous passage: وَٱلْحَافِظِينَ فُرُوجَهُمْ وَٱلْحَافِظَاتِ - those guarding their private parts, men constantly guarding and women constantly guarding.

The grammatical form is crucial. الْحَافِظِينَ is an active participle with the definite article - ism al-fa'il with alif-lam. This pattern creates emphasis and continuity. You get the present tense nature, the constancy, the ongoing action. But adding فُرُوجَهُمْ as the object takes it further - الْحَافِظِينَ فُرُوجَهُمْ implies they're constantly, vigilantly, continuously guarding. It's as though their desires, their temptations, their hormones, those thoughts are perpetually attacking them, and they're perpetually defending. Constant vigilance. Continuous guarding.

This applies to both men and women - both must constantly watch, defend, protect their chastity and character.

How do you maintain this constant vigilance? The answer comes immediately: وَٱلذَّاكِرِينَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱلذَّاكِرَاتِ - those who remember Allah much, constantly - men and women.

Notice the sequence: guarding the privates is mentioned first, then remembering Allah abundantly. If you examine the passage about the mothers of the believers, what was mentioned first? Guard yourselves - وَلَا تَبَرَّجْنَ (don't make tabarruj). Guard your chastity, your dignity, your modesty. Then what? وَٱذْكُرْنَ مَا يُتْلَىٰ فِى بُيُوتِكُنَّ (remember what is recited in your homes) - remembrance of Allah ﷻ.

The connection is explicit: How do you guard your shame? By remembering Allah ﷻ constantly. The weapon against temptation is continuous dhikr.

أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (Allah has prepared for them forgiveness and tremendous reward).

Why does this ayah about Muslim men and women in general appear right after the passage addressing the Prophet's ﷺ family and household? To remind us: this isn't just about them. They're your role models. You must guard your chastity too. You must remember Allah ﷻ constantly too. Just before, we were told the women of the Prophet's ﷺ household must guard themselves and remember Allah ﷻ - Quran is recited in their homes. Now we're told: as Muslims, you must do the same.

This is no accident. It's teaching methodology - establish the principle at the highest level with the role models, then immediately universalize it to all believers.

translation: It is not for a believing man or a believing woman, when Allah and His Messenger have decided a matter, that they should [thereafter] have any choice about their affair. And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly strayed into clear error
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ وَلَا مُؤْمِنَةٍ إِذَا قَضَى ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ أَمْرًا أَن يَكُونَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخِيَرَةُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِمْ ۗ وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًا مُّبِينًا

It is not for a believing man or believing woman, when Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ have decided a matter, that they should have any choice in their affair.

The word الْخِيَرَةُ  means choice, option, the ability to choose. When Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ have made a decision, have decreed something, have established a ruling - believers don't get to pick and choose. There's no option to say, "I prefer it this way" or "I think that would be better."

This isn't about matters where Islam gives options - those remain. This is about matters where Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ have clearly established a command or prohibition. In those matters, the believer's response is سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا (we hear and we obey), not "let me think about it" or "I'll choose what works for me."

وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَالًا مُّبِينًا (And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly strayed into clear error).

The emphasis in ضَلَّ ضَلَالًا - the verb and then the emphatic noun - indicates complete, obvious, undeniable misguidance. It's not a small mistake or minor deviation. It's manifest error, clear straying from the path.

This ayah comes at the end of this section as a foundational principle: when divine guidance is clear, the believer's role is submission, not selection. This is the essence of what it means to be Muslim - one who submits. The entire passage we've studied establishes this principle through the example of the Prophet's ﷺ household, then extends it to all believers, and now concludes with this explicit statement of the believer's position before divine decree.

translation: And [remember, O Muhammad], when you said to the one on whom Allah bestowed favor and you bestowed favor, "Keep your wife and fear Allah," while you concealed within yourself that which Allah is to disclose. And you feared the people, while Allah has more right that you fear Him. So when Zayd had no longer any need for her, We married her to you in order that there not be upon the believers any discomfort concerning the wives of their adopted sons when they no longer have need of them. And ever is the command of Allah accomplished
commentary:
وَإِذْ تَقُولُ لِلَّذِىٓ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَأَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَمْسِكْ عَلَيْكَ زَوْجَكَ وَٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ وَتُخْفِى فِى نَفْسِكَ مَا ٱللَّهُ مُبْدِيهِ وَتَخْشَى ٱلنَّاسَ وَٱللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَىٰهُ ۖ فَلَمَّا قَضَىٰ زَيْدٌ مِّنْهَا وَطَرًا زَوَّجْنَـٰكَهَا لِكَىْ لَا يَكُونَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ حَرَجٌ فِىٓ أَزْوَٰجِ أَدْعِيَآئِهِمْ إِذَا قَضَوْا۟ مِنْهُنَّ وَطَرًا ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا

This ayah addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly, recalling when he kept saying to the one whom Allah ﷻ had favored: "Hold on to your wife and be conscious of Allah." The description "the one Allah has favored" – الَّذِىٓ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ – is one of the great honors accorded to Zaid ibn Harithah (RA). How did Allah ﷻ favor him? He was raised in the family of the Prophet ﷺ, who loved him so much that he called him his own son – so much so that revelation had to come down clarifying that calling someone your son doesn't actually make him your son.

The phrase "and you favored him" – وَأَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِ – acknowledges that the Prophet ﷺ also showed Zaid (RA) tremendous love and preference in his life. When Zaid (RA) kept coming to the Prophet ﷺ saying the marriage wasn't working and asking if he could divorce, the Prophet ﷺ kept responding: أَمْسِكْ عَلَيْكَ زَوْجَكَ – "Hold on to your wife."

But the Prophet ﷺ wasn't just thinking of the immediate situation. He was considering the long-term implications. The entire Hashimi clan would be worried. Zainab (RA) would be heartbroken. The only way to truly address the situation would be for him to marry her himself. But he didn't know if that was a good idea because Zaid (RA) was his pronounced son, not his actual son by blood, but his pronounced son. This would give an opportunity for others to create a scandal. The entire web of events that were to come had already been figured out by the Prophet ﷺ, which is why he kept advising Zaid (RA) not to proceed with divorce.

The instruction "fear Allah" – اتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ – is significant here. It appears in the ayahs about divorce and troubled marriages every single time. When marriages deteriorate, that's when people are most likely to disobey Allah ﷻ. Things are said, actions are taken, harm is inflicted. The Prophet ﷺ was essentially saying: "I know things are difficult, but this is especially the time you need to be watchful of Allah."

Then Allah ﷻ addresses His Messenger ﷺ: وَتُخْفِى فِى نَفْسِكَ مَا ٱللَّهُ مُبْدِيهِ – "You were hiding within yourself what Allah was bent upon exposing." The entire marriage was going to dissolve, and eventually the Prophet ﷺ was going to marry Zainab (RA). This had already been revealed to the Prophet ﷺ, but he didn't want to say it because it sounded scandalous. Allah ﷻ uses the past tense (مُبْدِيهِ) because from His perspective, it was already a done deal.

وَتَخْشَى ٱلنَّاسَ – "You were fearing people" – meaning you were worried about what people might say. We must understand: the morale of the Muslim community rested on the dignity of the Prophet ﷺ. A scandal in his family, a scandal in his personal life, would hurt the morale of all of Islam. To this day, those who hate Islam – where do they attack? They attack the Prophet's ﷺ personal life. They raise issues about A'ishah (RA), about Zainab (RA), about Mariyah al-Qibtiyyah (RA). Why? Because that's where they can hurt Muslim morale the most.

One of the foundations of our religion is إِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ – "Indeed, you are of an incredible character and ethics." If someone can attack that, then one of the great foundations of the religion appears compromised. People's integrity comes first, before what they say. If you can raise questions about the Prophet's ﷺ character, his message means nothing to the doubter.

But Allah ﷻ says: وَٱللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَىٰهُ – "Allah is more deserving that you should fear Him." In other words: don't worry about people, just worry about obeying Me.

Then comes the specific mention: فَلَمَّا قَضَىٰ زَيْدٌ مِّنْهَا وَطَرًا – "When Zaid had concluded from her what he needed." This is the only time in the entire Quran where a companion is mentioned by name. There's no mention of Abu Bakr (RA) by name, no Umar (RA), no Uthman (RA), no Ali (RA), no A'ishah (RA) – only Zaid (RA). Why? Because this matter was so sensitive and so specific, it needed to be crystal clear. This cannot be generalized. This situation will never be duplicated. It's specific to the Prophet ﷺ and this particular marriage.

The word وَطَر literally means to share a bed, to share intimacy. Allah ﷻ is highlighting one of the fundamental problems in the marriage: intimacy was gone. When he was no longer sharing a bed with her, when they were not interested in each other, the marriage fell apart. These words don't appear in the Quran by accident. Allah ﷻ is teaching us one of the things that can make or destroy a marriage.

This connects to what was discussed earlier about the mothers of the believers being told not to beautify themselves in public. Let's consider the reality: in a society where women constantly beautify themselves at parties, gatherings, and workplaces, a husband is supposed to find beauty in his wife – that's what should be the case. He should be attracted to his wife. But in a society where women are constantly beautifying themselves everywhere, you cannot argue that he won't see other women he's attracted to, even if he can't help himself.

Even Prophet Yusuf (AS), a chaste and pure person, when that woman showed herself to him, had to seek refuge in Allah ﷻ because of desires placed within him that were beyond his control. Sometimes women struggle to understand this because they weren't created as men, and men struggle to understand women's psychology and emotions because we weren't created as women. We will never fully understand each other in some ways, and that's why we need Allah ﷻ to intervene.

When a young man comes to his mother saying he wants to get married, and she responds "What's the hurry?" – she doesn't know the fire burning inside him. She doesn't know the battle he goes through every day. Every time an attractive woman walks by, there's a literal battlefield in his mind. Shaytan says "Just take a look, just one look," and something inside him says "No, fear Allah." Sometimes he wins that battle, and many times he loses.

If society delays marriage until someone is 27 or older, he will have lost that battle so many times that even when he marries, he may not find satisfaction with his wife because his eyes haven't been safeguarded. These are real problems affecting real marriages today.

The husband and wife have to make an effort to be interested in each other. They have to be attracted to one another. The wife has to make sure the husband enjoys her company. If وَطَر – the sharing of the bed, the intimacy – is lost, then the marriage is effectively over.

زَوَّجْنَـٰكَهَا – "We married her to you" – Allah ﷻ directly tells the Prophet ﷺ: We performed your marriage. What was going to be a scandal became an honor. Allah ﷻ didn't just permit this marriage; He commanded it and executed it Himself through revelation. This took the burden completely off the Messenger ﷺ.

After her marriage to the Prophet ﷺ, Zainab (RA) would go out of her way to tell the other mothers of the believers: "Your families got you married, but my marriage was performed by Allah from above the seven heavens." This was revelation, and it became a point of honor for her.

The purpose is stated clearly: لِكَىْ لَا يَكُونَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ حَرَجٌ فِىٓ أَزْوَٰجِ أَدْعِيَآئِهِمْ – "So there would never be any discomfort upon the believers concerning the wives of their adopted sons." If someone called another person "my son" as a gesture of love because he's younger, but he's not actually his biological son, and that younger man divorces his wife, it's no problem for the older man to marry her. Allah ﷻ destroyed cultural taboos through this.

This also establishes that marriage across age gaps is not problematic in Islam. If there's a 45-year-old man who called a 30-year-old "son" out of affection, and that 30-year-old married someone and then divorced, there's no taboo if the 45-year-old later marries that divorced woman. Age gaps in marriage are not inherently wrong in our religion.

This doesn't justify corrupt practices in certain cultures where tribal leaders marry hundreds of teenage girls, use them for one night, divorce them, and ruin their lives while preventing them from marrying anyone else. That's filth and oppression. But in a normal, healthy setting, if there's a marriage need and you can't find a good prospect of similar age, it's acceptable in Islam to find an older or younger spouse. The conditions are decency, morality, and compatibility – not age.

If you have an older parent who is widowed and in their 50s or 60s and expresses interest in remarrying, don't stand in their way. We've made it humiliating for older people to even express this interest, but companionship is a human need that Islam doesn't deny at any age.

The ayah concludes: إِذَا قَضَوْا۟ مِنْهُنَّ وَطَرًا – when they have concluded from them that intimacy, when the marriage has ended. The expression emphasizes that marriage is supposed to be filled with passion and intimacy. وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا – "And the command of Allah is always executed."

translation: There is not to be upon the Prophet any discomfort concerning that which Allah has imposed upon him. [This is] the established way of Allah with those [prophets] who have passed on before. And ever is the command of Allah a destiny decreed
commentary:
مَّا كَانَ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيمَا فَرَضَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ ۖ سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ فِى ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ قَدَرًا مَّقْدُورًا

There is absolutely no blame upon the Prophet ﷺ in whatever Allah ﷻ has mandated for him. Allah ﷻ doesn't even say "permitted" – He says فَرَضَ, "mandated." This proves decisively that this marriage wasn't based on the Prophet's ﷺ personal interest or attraction. Allah ﷻ came to the defense of the Prophet's ﷺ character against any suggestion that he was attracted to someone else's wife. First, Allah ﷻ declared "We got you married." Second, "We mandated this marriage."

سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ فِى ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلُ – "This is the established way of Allah among those who passed before." This isn't something new. Prophets before faced similar situations and had to make difficult decisions that served the larger good of their communities.

وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ قَدَرًا مَّقْدُورًا – "The command of Allah is a decree determined." The word قَدَرًا indicates divine decree, and مَّقْدُورًا emphasizes it's decreed with finality – a final verdict that has been made and cannot be questioned.

translation: [Allah praises] those who convey the messages of Allah and fear Him and do not fear anyone but Allah. And sufficient is Allah as Accountant
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَخْشَوْنَهُۥ وَلَا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَدًا إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًا

The subject suddenly shifts to describe the true messengers: those who communicate the messages of Allah ﷻ and fear Him alone, not fearing anyone except Allah ﷻ. This is the actual mission of Islam – to share the message of Allah ﷻ.

But Allah ﷻ has tied these two things together: you won't be able to fulfill this mission of delivering Allah's message unless you have your social issues sorted out, your domestic issues resolved. You have to put the fire out in your own house first before you can help your neighbor put their fire out.

The messengers يُبَلِّغُونَ – they communicate, they convey رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ – Allah's messages. وَيَخْشَوْنَهُۥ – they fear Him with the deepest reverence. وَلَا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَدًا إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ – they don't fear anyone except Allah. This was the Prophet's ﷺ concern: he was afraid of people's reaction, of scandal. But Allah ﷻ is teaching: when you're on a mission from Allah ﷻ, fear only Him.

وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًا – "And sufficient is Allah as an Accountant" – Allah ﷻ is enough to take account of everything, to manage all affairs, to protect His messenger's ﷺ honor and reputation.

translation: Muhammad is not the father of [any] one of your men, but [he is] the Messenger of Allah and last of the prophets. And ever is Allah, of all things, Knowing
commentary:
مَّا كَانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبَآ أَحَدٍ مِّن رِّجَالِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن رَّسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَخَاتَمَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا

Muhammad ﷺ is not the father of any one of your men. This definitively ends the practice of calling Zaid (RA) "Zaid ibn Muhammad." You will never be able to say that again.

The context is important: Mariyah al-Qibtiyyah (RA) was originally Egyptian, from the Coptic community. This parallels how Hajar (AS), the wife of Ibrahim (AS), was also from Egypt, and from her came Ismail (AS) and his descendants. When Mariyah (RA) was given as a gift to the Prophet ﷺ by the Muqawqis of Egypt, she bore him a son whom he named Ibrahim (AS) – connecting to that similar Egyptian lineage.

The Prophet ﷺ was deeply attached to his son Ibrahim (AS). When Ibrahim passed away as an infant, the Prophet ﷺ said, "O Ibrahim, we are very sad at your parting." He was profoundly connected to Ibrahim (AS).

But Allah ﷻ declares: مَّا كَانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبَآ أَحَدٍ مِّن رِّجَالِكُمْ – Muhammad is not the father of any one of your men. He had no surviving male offspring who reached adulthood.

وَلَـٰكِن رَّسُولَ ٱللَّهِ – However, he is the Messenger of Allah. وَخَاتَمَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ – and the seal, the final of all prophets.

The word خَاتَم means seal, like the seal at the end of a letter. When you say "Ameen" at the end of Surah al-Fatihah, that's the seal, the final statement – nothing comes after it. خَاتَمَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ – the seal of all prophets, meaning no prophet comes after him ﷺ.

This is crucial because there are no more role models coming after the Prophet ﷺ. He even had to make these difficult decisions about complicated marriages because he had to leave a legacy behind. Nobody after him can establish those precedents. He was put in these positions so the Ummah would have guidance for every situation.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا – "And Allah has always been completely knowledgeable of all things."

translation: O you who have believed, remember Allah with much remembrance
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ذِكْرًا كَثِيرًا

Now the address shifts to the believers: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ – O you who have believed. ٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ذِكْرًا كَثِيرًا – remember Allah with abundant remembrance.

The instruction comes precisely here because people might be preoccupied with gossip about these marriages: "So he got married to her, huh? What's going on there?" Allah ﷻ is redirecting the believers: don't fill your minds with scandal and small talk. Don't be preoccupied with analyzing other people's private affairs. Replace that time with abundant dhikr of Allah ﷻ.

This echoes what we learned in the previous Surah, Sajdah, and before that in Surah Luqman: be busy declaring the perfection of Allah ﷻ all the time. Some people were trying to raise this situation as a scandal, so Allah ﷻ found the most dignified way of telling the Muslims: you need to be preoccupied with something more productive. Engage in dhikr of Allah ﷻ and tasbih of Allah ﷻ.

If you're sitting with religiously mature people and someone starts talking negatively about a marriage, divorce, or personal scandal, you'll notice the mature person sitting in the corner will start saying SubhanAllah, Alhamdulillah, La ilaha illAllah. They're doing their job because they know what dhikr means in this context. These people are talking trash, and I don't want to waste my time with that. I'd rather remember Allah ﷻ at this time. If you're smart, you'll get the hint from what they're saying.

translation: And exalt Him morning and afternoon
commentary:
وَسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا

وَسَبِّحُوهُ – and glorify Him, declare His perfection. بُكْرَةً – in the morning. وَأَصِيلًا – and in the evening.

This isn't just ritual remembrance – it's a way of life. Start your day with the remembrance of Allah ﷻ, end your day with the remembrance of Allah ﷻ. Keep your tongue engaged in His glorification morning and evening.

translation: It is He who confers blessing upon you, and His angels [ask Him to do so] that He may bring you out from darknesses into the light. And ever is He, to the believers, Merciful
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُصَلِّى عَلَيْكُمْ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتُهُۥ لِيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۚ وَكَانَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَحِيمًا

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُصَلِّى عَلَيْكُمْ – He is the One who sends blessings upon you, who prays for you, who mentions you with honor. وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتُهُۥ – and His angels do the same.

The purpose: لِيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ – so that He can bring you out of darkness into light. When Allah ﷻ remembers you, when He sends His blessings upon you, when the angels pray for you, the result is that you're pulled from darkness into light – from confusion to clarity, from sin to righteousness, from despair to hope.

وَكَانَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَحِيمًا – "And He has always been especially merciful to the believers." When it specifically comes to believers, Allah ﷻ is extremely loving, compassionate, and merciful.

translation: Their greeting the Day they meet Him will be, "Peace." And He has prepared for them a noble reward
commentary:
تَحِيَّتُهُمْ يَوْمَ يَلْقَوْنَهُۥ سَلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا كَرِيمًا

تَحِيَّتُهُمْ يَوْمَ يَلْقَوْنَهُۥ سَلَـٰمٌ – Their greeting on the Day they meet Him will be "Salaam."

Think about the etiquette of salaam in this world: the younger should say salaam to the elder, the one arriving should greet those already present, the child should greet the parent, the follower should greet the leader. The one who says salaam first is acknowledging the higher position of the other. The one who responds to salaam is in the position of honor.

How elevated does Allah ﷻ raise believers on the Day of Judgment? We don't even get to say salaam first – He says it to us! سَلَـٰمٌ – Allah ﷻ will say "Peace" to the believers. This is an unimaginable honor.

وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا كَرِيمًا – "And He has prepared for them a noble, gracious compensation." Not just any reward, but a كَرِيم reward – one that is generous, dignified, noble.

translation: O Prophet, indeed We have sent you as a witness and a bringer of good tidings and a warner
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ شَـٰهِدًا وَمُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا

Now Allah ﷻ directly addresses His Prophet ﷺ: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ – O Prophet. إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ – Indeed, We have sent you.

شَـٰهِدًا – as a witness. The Prophet ﷺ will witness what people did with this revelation. He will witness how they behaved with him. He will testify before Allah ﷻ about what the hypocrites did with the Quran and what the believers did with the Quran. Because of that testimony, Allah ﷻ says:

وَمُبَشِّرًا – and as a giver of good news. If the Prophet ﷺ testifies for you, that's good news.

وَنَذِيرًا – and as a warner. If he testifies against you, that's a warning. The Prophet ﷺ plays both roles: someone who comes to court to testify for you, and someone who comes to court to testify against you.

This should make us reflect: which group will we be in? Those the Prophet ﷺ testifies for, or those he testifies against?

translation: And one who invites to Allah, by His permission, and an illuminating lamp
commentary:
وَدَاعِيًا إِلَى ٱللَّهِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ وَسِرَاجًا مُّنِيرًا

وَدَاعِيًا إِلَى ٱللَّهِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ – and as a caller to Allah by His permission. Not just by His permission, but بِإِذْنِهِۦ – by His sanction, His authorization, His command. Allah ﷻ sanctioned him, mandated him to call people to the path of Allah ﷻ.

وَسِرَاجًا مُّنِيرًا – and as a brilliant, illuminating lamp.

This description is beautiful. Earlier in the Quran, in Surah al-Furqan, the moon was described as نُورًا (light) and the sun was described as سِرَاجًا (a lamp). The sun is hard to look at directly, but the moon is pleasing to observe. You can drive around, see a full moon and think, "Wow, that's amazing." Nobody looks up at the sun and says, "Ah, how beautiful!"

Allah ﷻ took the calm brilliance of the moon and the intensity of the sun and combined them to describe the Prophet ﷺ. He is سِرَاجًا مُّنِيرًا – a lamp that gives constant light like the sun, but soothing and accessible light like the moon. He's constantly giving guidance, but in a way that's gentle and approachable.

translation: And give good tidings to the believers that they will have from Allah great bounty
commentary:
وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ فَضْلًا كَبِيرًا

وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ – Give good news to those who truly believe. بِأَنَّ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ فَضْلًا كَبِيرًا – that for them from Allah is immense favor.

Notice where this ayah comes in the sequence. The previous ayahs commanded abundant dhikr of Allah ﷻ, declaring His perfection morning and evening. What's the easiest way to build the favor of Allah ﷻ in your life? Sending salah upon the Prophet ﷺ: اللهم صل على محمد وعلى آل محمد كما صليت على إبراهيم وعلى آل إبراهيم – with every salah upon the Prophet ﷺ, we're building our rewards in Paradise.

فَضْل is different from other words for reward. أَجْر means compensation – you did something, you get paid accordingly. ثَوَاب means good reward for good deeds. جَزَاء means full, complete compensation. But فَضْل means bonus, something extra, something you didn't even earn.

Allah ﷻ will give believers things they haven't even earned. And it's not just فَضْل – it's فَضْلًا كَبِيرًا – immense favor, huge bonus. When you get a bonus at work, it might be 5% of your salary, maybe 10%, and if it's a really generous company, perhaps 20%. But this is فَضْلًا كَبِيرًا – as if your actual salary won't even compare to the bonus. The extra will be huge.

translation: And do not obey the disbelievers and the hypocrites but do not harm them, and rely upon Allah. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs
commentary:
وَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَدَعْ أَذَىٰهُمْ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا

This connects back to the beginning of these passages. Earlier Allah ﷻ said: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ – "O Prophet, be conscious of Allah and don't obey the disbelievers and hypocrites."

Now Allah ﷻ repeats: وَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ – Don't be influenced by the disbelievers who will say "How could he marry his pronounced son's ex-wife?" And don't listen to the hypocrites who will raise other issues to create scandal.

وَدَعْ أَذَىٰهُمْ – and abandon, leave alone their hurtful words. أَذَى doesn't mean physical harm so much as pinch, sting, hurtful words. We learned this back in Surah Ali Imran: لَن يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّا أَذًى – "They cannot harm you except with hurtful words."

The Prophet ﷺ was being prepared. He was afraid that a scandal might start that would hurt him and hurt Islam. What the Prophet ﷺ didn't even know yet when this was revealed is that the scandal of the ifk – the false accusation against A'ishah (RA) – was around the corner. But the Prophet ﷺ was already being prepared: don't worry about the hurtful things they say.

وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ – Place your trust completely in Allah ﷻ. وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا – and sufficient is Allah as the One to take care of your affairs, as your Protector, as your Guardian.

translation: O You who have believed, when you marry believing women and then divorce them before you have touched them, then there is not for you any waiting period to count concerning them. So provide for them and give them a gracious release
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا نَكَحْتُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَمَسُّوهُنَّ فَمَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِنْ عِدَّةٍ تَعْتَدُّونَهَا ۖ فَمَتِّعُوهُنَّ وَسَرِّحُوهُنَّ سَرَاحًا جَمِيلًا

Now the address shifts from specific policies for the Prophet ﷺ to general guidance for all believers: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ – O you who have believed.

إِذَا نَكَحْتُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ – When you marry believing women. ثُمَّ طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَمَسُّوهُنَّ – and then you divorce them before you have touched them, before you have consummated the marriage.

In other words, you got married and she hasn't even moved into your house yet. You had the ceremony, families came together, but before any physical contact or consummation, something happens – you talked a couple of times and realized you're incompatible, or things spiraled out of control somehow.

فَمَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِنْ عِدَّةٍ تَعْتَدُّونَهَا – then you don't owe against them, and you don't have against them any expectation of waiting period.

Normally, when divorce happens, there's a three-month iddah period. This happens for several reasons: the divorce may not be final, allowing time for reconciliation. Also, if the wife became pregnant, the original husband has a right to know. Within three months it will be clearly known if she's carrying a child. During these three months, she's not supposed to accept any marriage proposals or consider other relationships. After three months, she can move on with her life.

But if you never had physical contact with her, then there's no question of pregnancy, so that rule doesn't apply anymore.

The instruction continues: فَمَتِّعُوهُنَّ – then give them gifts. You married her and it was a significant event. Families came together, ceremonies happened. It's natural that she started becoming emotionally attached to her fiancé. Days and weeks before, the thought of marriage made her happy. Then suddenly it's stripped from her – that's psychologically traumatic.

So you need to give her gifts as compensation out of your own goodness and generosity. وَسَرِّحُوهُنَّ سَرَاحًا جَمِيلًا – and let them go, set them free in the most beautiful way. Be dignified about it. Don't say hurtful words, don't let your frustrations out. Just be dignified in the way you end things when the marriage dissolves.

translation: O Prophet, indeed We have made lawful to you your wives to whom you have given their due compensation and those your right hand possesses from what Allah has returned to you [of captives] and the daughters of your paternal uncles and the daughters of your paternal aunts and the daughters of your maternal uncles and the daughters of your maternal aunts who emigrated with you and a believing woman if she gives herself to the Prophet [and] if the Prophet wishes to marry her, [this is] only for you, excluding the [other] believers. We certainly know what We have made obligatory upon them concerning their wives and those their right hands possess, [but this is for you] in order that there will be upon you no discomfort. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِنَّآ أَحْلَلْنَا لَكَ أَزْوَٰجَكَ ٱلَّـٰتِىٓ ءَاتَيْتَ أُجُورَهُنَّ وَمَا مَلَكَتْ يَمِينُكَ مِمَّآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ وَبَنَاتِ عَمِّكَ وَبَنَاتِ عَمَّـٰتِكَ وَبَنَاتِ خَالِكَ وَبَنَاتِ خَـٰلَـٰتِكَ ٱلَّـٰتِى هَاجَرْنَ مَعَكَ وَٱمْرَأَةً مُّؤْمِنَةً إِن وَهَبَتْ نَفْسَهَا لِلنَّبِىِّ إِنْ أَرَادَ ٱلنَّبِىُّ أَن يَسْتَنكِحَهَا خَالِصَةً لَّكَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۗ قَدْ عَلِمْنَا مَا فَرَضْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِىٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَمَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ لِكَيْلَا يَكُونَ عَلَيْكَ حَرَجٌ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

The address returns specifically to the Prophet ﷺ: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ – O Prophet.

إِنَّآ أَحْلَلْنَا لَكَ أَزْوَٰجَكَ – Indeed, We have made permissible for you your wives. Not a single one of your marriages is questionable. Don't worry about any of them.

ٱلَّـٰتِىٓ ءَاتَيْتَ أُجُورَهُنَّ – those to whom you have given their compensations. Allah ﷻ is calling mahr as though it's a payment that must be given. The dowry, the marriage gift – it has to be given. It's mandated even on the Prophet ﷺ.

وَمَا مَلَكَتْ يَمِينُكَ مِمَّآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ – And in addition, whatever your right hand possesses from what Allah has returned to you. This refers to Mariyah al-Qibtiyyah (RA) and possibly Rayhana (RA), who came through fay' (war returns).

وَبَنَاتِ عَمِّكَ – And the daughters of your paternal uncles (your father's brothers' daughters).

وَبَنَاتِ عَمَّـٰتِكَ – And the daughters of your paternal aunts (your father's sisters' daughters).

وَبَنَاتِ خَالِكَ – And the daughters of your maternal uncles (your mother's brothers' daughters).

وَبَنَاتِ خَـٰلَـٰتِكَ – And the daughters of your maternal aunts (your mother's sisters' daughters).

ٱلَّـٰتِى هَاجَرْنَ مَعَكَ – specifically those who migrated with you. This is an important condition – the permissibility extended to those who made hijrah.

وَٱمْرَأَةً مُّؤْمِنَةً إِن وَهَبَتْ نَفْسَهَا لِلنَّبِىِّ – And any believing woman if she gave herself as a gift to the Prophet. In other words, she married him without demanding mahr, saying "I don't want any mahr – you being my husband is mahr enough."

إِنْ أَرَادَ ٱلنَّبِىُّ أَن يَسْتَنكِحَهَا – if the Prophet wanted to marry her. This is significant: it's not mandated. Allah ﷻ isn't saying that every time a woman comes to the Prophet ﷺ offering to marry without mahr, he must accept. The Prophet ﷺ might have felt obligated otherwise, thinking revelation required him to accept. But Allah ﷻ clarifies: this is up to you. The marriage We mandated was Zainab (RA) – that's done, that's finished. This option is purely for you to decide.

خَالِصَةً لَّكَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ – This is purely for you, exclusive to you, not for other believers. For everyone else, mahr is required. Don't say "I love you so much, I don't want any mahr." No, take mahr. And if she's not smart enough to ask for something appropriate, at least ask for something symbolic – a penny, something. Then negotiate something decent, something she can survive on for a few months at minimum, should things not work out.

قَدْ عَلِمْنَا مَا فَرَضْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِىٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ – We already know what We have mandated on them regarding their spouses. The laws of marriage have already been revealed in Surah an-Nisa, the laws of divorce in Surah al-Baqarah. That's already taken care of, already established.

لِكَيْلَا يَكُونَ عَلَيْكَ حَرَجٌ – so that you don't have any restriction, any hardship, any discomfort upon you.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا – And Allah has always been exceedingly forgiving, always merciful.

translation: You, [O Muhammad], may put aside whom you will of them or take to yourself whom you will. And any that you desire of those [wives] from whom you had [temporarily] separated - there is no blame upon you [in returning her]. That is more suitable that they should be content and not grieve and that they should be satisfied with what you have given them - all of them. And Allah knows what is in your hearts. And ever is Allah Knowing and Forbearing
commentary:
۞ تُرْجِى مَن تَشَآءُ مِنْهُنَّ وَتُـْٔوِىٓ إِلَيْكَ مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ وَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَيْتَ مِمَّنْ عَزَلْتَ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن تَقَرَّ أَعْيُنُهُنَّ وَلَا يَحْزَنَّ وَيَرْضَيْنَ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْتَهُنَّ كُلُّهُنَّ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَلِيمًا

This is another unique dispensation given specifically to the Prophet ﷺ regarding his multiple wives.

تُرْجِى مَن تَشَآءُ مِنْهُنَّ – You may defer, put off visiting whichever of them you wish. The word تُرْجِى means to postpone something good that's coming to you. Like when you buy your favorite chocolate and think "I'll save this for later, I'll have it on a special day." You're putting it off, but it's yours, it's good, you want it – just later.

Allah ﷻ is saying the Prophet ﷺ can treat his wives this way. He might think "I want to go see her, but I have other priorities today, so I'm going to put off meeting her. I'm not going to visit her house today."

وَتُـْٔوِىٓ إِلَيْكَ مَن تَشَآءُ – And you can bring close to yourself, take refuge with whomever you want. You can be with whoever you want and spend as much time as you want with them.

Normally, you would think a man with multiple wives is obligated to give them exactly equal time. In fact, we learn that the Prophet ﷺ by his own choice did give them equal time out of his excellence. But Allah ﷻ is saying he's not actually obligated to – he could exercise discretion based on the demands of his mission.

وَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَيْتَ مِمَّنْ عَزَلْتَ – And if you were to pursue one that you've set aside for a while. Maybe you got busy with battles, with delegation meetings, with revelation, with teaching, and you didn't make time for one of them. Maybe she feels abandoned.

فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكَ – Then there's no blame on you. It's probably better, recommended that you go back to spend time with her so she doesn't feel neglected, but even if you don't, there's no sin on you.

This shows how much trust Allah ﷻ has in the Messenger's ﷺ excellence as a husband. Allah ﷻ doesn't have to spell out for him that he must make time for his spouses. He's given open discretion, and even though he has that freedom, he doesn't abuse it.

For the rest of the believers, the ayah came earlier: "If you cannot be absolutely just, then marry only one." But for the Prophet ﷺ, "It's all permissible for you, you have open discretion, because I know you have a very important mission. You've got priorities. Maybe you won't be able to make time for all of them equally – it's understandable for you."

ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن تَقَرَّ أَعْيُنُهُنَّ – That is more likely that their eyes will be cooled, comforted. When you do visit after being away, they're looking at you saying "I missed you, you should come more often" and they become calm again.

وَلَا يَحْزَنَّ – And so they don't become overwhelmed with sadness from your absence.

وَيَرْضَيْنَ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْتَهُنَّ كُلُّهُنَّ – And so they all become pleased with what you have given to them – in terms of your company, your time, the love you've shown them.

وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ – And Allah knows whatever is in your hearts. He knows the Prophet's ﷺ intentions, his priorities, his struggles to balance his mission with his family responsibilities.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَلِيمًا – And Allah has always been fully knowledgeable and forbearing, patient, understanding.

translation: Not lawful to you, [O Muhammad], are [any additional] women after [this], nor [is it] for you to exchange them for [other] wives, even if their beauty were to please you, except what your right hand possesses. And ever is Allah, over all things, an Observer
commentary:
لَّا يَحِلُّ لَكَ ٱلنِّسَآءُ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَلَآ أَن تَبَدَّلَ بِهِنَّ مِنْ أَزْوَٰجٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ حُسْنُهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا مَلَكَتْ يَمِينُكَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ رَّقِيبًا

Then this final restriction came down specifically for the Prophet ﷺ: لَّا يَحِلُّ لَكَ ٱلنِّسَآءُ مِنۢ بَعْدُ – Marrying women after this point is no longer permissible for you. Zainab (RA) was the last one. The chapter on marriage is now closed for you.

وَلَآ أَن تَبَدَّلَ بِهِنَّ مِنْ أَزْوَٰجٍ – And you can no longer replace any of your spouses. You cannot divorce any of them and marry someone else. The chapter on both marriage and divorce is sealed for the Prophet ﷺ.

وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ حُسْنُهُنَّ – And even if their beauty impresses you. Even if their character – حُسْن also means physical beauty, there's no denying it – even if their beauty catches your attention.

This is profound. The Prophet ﷺ, the one with hayaa, the one who teaches us to lower our gaze, the one who with just a first glance would never look again – even if that one inadvertent glance finds someone beautiful, he cannot pursue them anymore.

What are we learning here? If the Prophet ﷺ, with that many spouses, can still find someone beautiful with an accidental glance, then we're certainly not immune. This is a fact of life that has to be dealt with honestly.

This is why, as difficult as it is to say, the wife has to maintain her husband's attention. She's competing with the fitna outside. She has to keep her husband interested through her appearance, her character, her company. This doesn't mean the husband has no obligations – he absolutely does, and those are addressed elsewhere in the Quran. But right now, this particular reality is being acknowledged.

Neither husband nor wife should be defensive when they hear their obligations. If someone tells you to do something that applies to you, don't deflect by saying "What about the other side?" If it applies to you, you have to carry your own burdens. Your obligation is to Allah ﷻ. Whether your spouse does what they're supposed to or not, you're going to do what you're supposed to – not for them, but for Allah ﷻ.

إِلَّا مَا مَلَكَتْ يَمِينُكَ – Except what your right hand already possesses. Mariyah (RA) and Rayhana (RA) – that's established. And if something else comes that way through legitimate means, that's fine. But no more new marriages beyond this.

By the way, there were only two in this category for the Prophet ﷺ.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ رَّقِيبًا – And Allah is completely and thoroughly and carefully watchful over all things, over every single thing.

translation: O you who have believed, do not enter the houses of the Prophet except when you are permitted for a meal, without awaiting its readiness. But when you are invited, then enter; and when you have eaten, disperse without seeking to remain for conversation. Indeed, that [behavior] was troubling the Prophet, and he is shy of [dismissing] you. But Allah is not shy of the truth. And when you ask [his wives] for something, ask them from behind a partition. That is purer for your hearts and their hearts. And it is not [conceivable or lawful] for you to harm the Messenger of Allah or to marry his wives after him, ever. Indeed, that would be in the sight of Allah an enormity
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ إِلَّآ أَن يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ إِلَىٰ طَعَامٍ غَيْرَ نَـٰظِرِينَ إِنَىٰهُ وَلَـٰكِنْ إِذَا دُعِيتُمْ فَٱدْخُلُوا۟ فَإِذَا طَعِمْتُمْ فَٱنتَشِرُوا۟ وَلَا مُسْتَـْٔنِسِينَ لِحَدِيثٍ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكُمْ كَانَ يُؤْذِى ٱلنَّبِىَّ فَيَسْتَحْىِۦ مِنكُمْ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَسْتَحْىِۦ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ وَإِذَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُنَّ مَتَـٰعًا فَسْـَٔلُوهُنَّ مِن وَرَآءِ حِجَابٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَطْهَرُ لِقُلُوبِكُمْ وَقُلُوبِهِنَّ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَن تُؤْذُوا۟ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَآ أَن تَنكِحُوٓا۟ أَزْوَٰجَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكُمْ كَانَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَظِيمًا

Now come instructions about proper etiquette in the Prophet's ﷺ household, though these manners should really be taught and learned by all believers.

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ – O you who have believed.

لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ – Don't enter the homes of the Prophet. He doesn't have one home – he has multiple homes for his multiple wives. He's with this one, that one, another one.

إِلَّآ أَن يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ – Don't enter any of them until and unless permission is granted to you. Don't just walk in. We've already been taught in Surah an-Nur about asking permission.

إِلَىٰ طَعَامٍ – or unless you've been called to a meal. The Prophet ﷺ sometimes called companions to lunch or dinner at his place to discuss matters.

How big were these houses? We're talking about one room – that's it. When a guest came in and sat with the Prophet ﷺ, A'ishah (RA) would simply turn around with her hijab and sit facing the wall until the guest left. There was nowhere else to go. We're not even talking about 100 square feet – probably more like 70 square feet, smaller than many modern bathrooms.

غَيْرَ نَـٰظِرِينَ إِنَىٰهُ – Don't show up early and just wait around for the food to be ready. Don't arrive before it's time. If iftar is at 8 o'clock, don't show up at 7. The family needs time to prepare. Don't just appear and sit there waiting while food is being cooked.

We learned in Surah an-Nur, which was revealed later, that if people show up at an inappropriate time, you have the right to send them back home. You can say "Please don't come right now, we're busy," and they shouldn't take offense.

وَلَـٰكِنْ إِذَا دُعِيتُمْ فَٱدْخُلُوا۟ – But when you are invited, then enter.

فَإِذَا طَعِمْتُمْ فَٱنتَشِرُوا۟ – And as soon as you're done eating, disperse, leave, get out of there. The Prophet ﷺ can't say that directly – he's too gracious. He won't just get up and say "Okay, it was nice seeing you, there's the door." He'll just sit there waiting for you to realize it's time to go.

So Allah ﷻ does it for him: Don't just sit there after eating. The Prophet ﷺ has family, he has more issues to deal with than any other human being on the planet. Respect his time. Eat your food and leave.

What do we do at people's dinner invitations? We eat, and then at midnight someone finally says "Yeah, we should get going" – and 45 minutes later, still sitting there. Then you tell the wife "Chalo" (Let's go) – and an hour later, "Ch

translation: Whether you reveal a thing or conceal it, indeed Allah is ever, of all things, Knowing

There is no blame upon women concerning their fathers or their sons or their brothers or their brothers' sons or their sisters' sons or their women or those their right hands possess. And fear Allah. Indeed Allah is ever, over all things, Witness
commentary:
لَّا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِنَّ فِىٓ ءَابَآئِهِنَّ وَلَآ أَبْنَآئِهِنَّ وَلَآ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ وَلَآ أَبْنَآءِ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ وَلَآ أَبْنَآءِ أَخَوَٰتِهِنَّ وَلَا نِسَآئِهِنَّ وَلَا مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُنَّ ۗ وَٱتَّقِينَ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدًا

This ayah establishes exceptions to the barrier requirement mentioned earlier in the surah. While the previous ayahs commanded specific precautions in interactions with the wives of the Prophet ﷺ, this ayah clarifies which family members are exempt from these restrictions.

There is no harm upon them in their interaction with their fathers, nor with their sons, nor their brothers, nor their nephews from their brothers' side, nor their nephews from their sisters' side, nor their own women, nor those whom their right hands possess. The list encompasses the mahram relationships where the usual barriers need not be maintained.

The phrase وَلَا نِسَآئِهِنَّ (nor their own women) has been understood in multiple ways by the scholars. It can refer to other Muslim women in the family, such as cousins, or more broadly to all Muslim women. The phrase وَلَا مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُنَّ (nor those whom their right hands possess) refers to their slaves or servants.

However, the ayah concludes with a critical reminder: وَٱتَّقِينَ ٱللَّهَ (and maintain consciousness of Allah ﷻ). Even among these close family members, taqwa must be observed. The command to maintain God-consciousness means that even in the absence of physical barriers, there should still be dignity, proper conduct and appropriate boundaries. For instance, once siblings reach a certain age, there should be natural separation and respect for privacy. The Prophet ﷺ recommended separating children in their sleeping arrangements as they mature.

The ayah ends with إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدًا (Indeed, Allah ﷻ has always been a complete witness over all things). Allah ﷻ sees everything, whether it occurs behind barriers or in the presence of mahram relatives. This Divine witnessing should influence how we conduct ourselves in all situations.

translation: Indeed, Allah confers blessing upon the Prophet, and His angels [ask Him to do so]. O you who have believed, ask [Allah to confer] blessing upon him and ask [Allah to grant him] peace
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتَهُۥ يُصَلُّونَ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ ۚ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ صَلُّوا۟ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلِّمُوا۟ تَسْلِيمًا

Allah ﷻ Himself and His angels send prayers upon the Prophet ﷺ. When the Creator of the universe honors His messenger in this way, what should be the response of those who claim to believe? يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ صَلُّوا۟ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلِّمُوا۟ تَسْلِيمًا (O you who have believed, send prayers upon him and greetings of peace).

The word تَسْلِيمًا  carries profound meaning. The root س-ل-م relates to peace, safety and submission. When we say "As-salamu alaykum," we're offering peace. But taslim also means complete submission. This is illustrated in Surah Al-Ahzab itself, where the believers see the approaching enemy army and respond with increased faith and submission: وَلَمَّا رَأَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَصَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ وَمَا زَادَهُمْ إِلَّآ إِيمَـٰنًا وَتَسْلِيمًا (When the believers saw the confederates, they said, "This is what Allah ﷻ and His Messenger promised us, and Allah ﷻ and His Messenger spoke the truth." And it increased them only in faith and submission) (33:22).

Therefore, sending salawat upon the Prophet ﷺ is not merely reciting "Allahumma salli ala Muhammad." It means submitting completely to his instructions and applying them in our lives. After all these ayahs about proper conduct, modesty and family relations, we're being told that true salawat upon the Prophet ﷺ includes giving in to his guidance.

translation: Indeed, those who abuse Allah and His Messenger - Allah has cursed them in this world and the Hereafter and prepared for them a humiliating punishment
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا

Those who cause harm to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ—Allah ﷻ has cursed them in this world and the Hereafter, and He has prepared for them humiliating punishment. This ayah came down about those who tried to hurt the Messenger of Allah ﷺ through slander, false accusations and attempts to damage his reputation and mission.

Consider the gravity of this: these ayahs were revealed, yet Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the chief hypocrite, continued his campaign against the Prophet ﷺ and the believers. Even after the explicit curse and warning of humiliating punishment, he persisted in his harm. This reveals the depth of his hypocrisy and arrogance.

translation: And those who harm believing men and believing women for [something] other than what they have earned have certainly born upon themselves a slander and manifest sin
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ بِغَيْرِ مَا ٱكْتَسَبُوا۟ فَقَدِ ٱحْتَمَلُوا۟ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا

The warning extends beyond harming the Prophet ﷺ. Those who cause harm to believing men and women بِغَيْرِ مَا ٱكْتَسَبُوا۟ (without them having earned it)—they have certainly carried upon themselves a slander and clear sin.

This ayah addresses the casual way people damage reputations. Someone sits in a gathering and mentions something negative about a believer—starting rumors, spreading gossip, talking negatively about families or individuals in the community. "I heard this person is like this," or "Did you know about what's happening with that family?" These casual conversations about people who haven't actually done anything wrong fall under this severe warning.

The phrase بِغَيْرِ مَا ٱكْتَسَبُوا۟ is critical. It means they're hurting people's reputations when those people haven't actually earned that criticism. You heard something and passed it along. You made an assumption and shared it. The accusation itself might be unclear—maybe it happened, maybe it didn't. But what will be crystal clear on the Day of Judgment is that you started or spread that rumor. There will be no ambiguity about your guilt in that regard.

translation: O Prophet, tell your wives and your daughters and the women of the believers to bring down over themselves [part] of their outer garments. That is more suitable that they will be known and not be abused. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ قُل لِّأَزْوَٰجِكَ وَبَنَاتِكَ وَنِسَآءِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يُدْنِينَ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِن جَلَـٰبِيبِهِنَّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن يُعْرَفْنَ فَلَا يُؤْذَيْنَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

Allah ﷻ now addresses the Prophet ﷺ with instructions for dress code. The command begins with his family: قُل لِّأَزْوَٰجِكَ وَبَنَاتِكَ (Tell your wives and your daughters). Allah ﷻ could have simply said "tell the women of the believers," which would include everyone. But by starting with the Prophet's family, the ayah emphasizes that change begins at home. Throughout this surah, the family of the Prophet ﷺ has been the primary focus, but now these rules extend to all believing women.

Let's examine the key terms carefully. The word يُدْنِينَ comes from the root د-ن-ا, which means to bring something close and to lower it. In Surah Ar-Rahman, the massive trees of Paradise are described as دَانِيَةً—their branches lower themselves close to you, making it easy to pick the fruit. So يُدْنِينَ carries two meanings: to bring close and to lower or drop down.

The instruction is يُدْنِينَ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِن جَلَـٰبِيبِهِنَّ (let them draw over themselves from their jilbabs). The word أَدْنَىٰ relates to draping or dropping something over yourself. When you put on a shirt, it drops and drapes over your body. When someone puts on a long dress, it falls and drapes naturally.

This tells us several things about the garment being described. First, it cannot be tight, because a tight garment doesn't drop or drape—it clings. Second, it suggests a loose, flowing garment that falls naturally when worn. Third, since it's described as coming عَلَيْهِنَّ (over them), it comes from above, meaning it starts from the head region.

Now we must understand جَلَـٰبِيبِ. A jilbab is a large outer garment. To put this in perspective, if you were wearing a smaller cloth that covered up to your belly, that would be called a khimar. The jilbab, however, is a much larger garment that covers from head to toe.

It's important to note that the head covering was already mentioned in Surah An-Nur, where the khimar is to be drawn over the chest, ensuring the hair doesn't show and the chest is covered in a loose way. This ayah in Al-Ahzab adds an additional layer—an outer garment that drapes over the entire body.

Different cultures in the Muslim world have manifested this in various ways. Some have the head covering and outer garment as separate pieces. Others have combined them into one flowing garment with an opening for the eyes. The key principle is that it must be loose enough to drape, and it must cover the body in a way that doesn't reveal the form.

There has been extensive scholarly discussion about the precise meaning of this ayah. Among the Companions (RA) themselves, there were varying interpretations. For instance, Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA), who had been a rabbi before accepting Islam, held the most conservative view—that even a woman's pinky showing was equivalent to displaying beauty. This came from his particular rabbinic tradition where women's modesty standards were extremely strict.

Other Companions (RA) understood this ayah in light of the earlier revelation in Surah An-Nur, concluding that we must combine both sets of instructions. What's particularly noteworthy is that this ayah in Al-Ahzab came first chronologically, and Surah An-Nur came later, providing further clarification.

The niqab (face veil) has been a matter of scholarly debate throughout Islamic history. Some scholars considered it mandatory, others recommended, and others said it was cultural rather than religious. Female scholars have held positions across this spectrum as well.

What's critical to understand is that this is a discussion that should be had with qualified scholars of Islamic jurisprudence who understand the detailed evidence and the positions of the classical scholars. In modern times, this has become unnecessarily controversial, with some people claiming it's entirely foreign to Islam, and others saying it's absolutely mandatory with no room for discussion. The reality is that there is legitimate scholarly difference on this matter.

The ayah explains the wisdom: ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن يُعْرَفْنَ فَلَا يُؤْذَيْنَ (That is more likely that they will be recognized and not be harmed). When women are recognized as Muslims who observe modesty, they will be left alone and treated with the respect their faith demands.

This is crucial: some people today argue, "Don't wear hijab or outer garments because you'll be targeted." The ayah uses the passive form فَلَا يُؤْذَيْنَ (they will not be harmed), which doesn't specify who might cause harm. The harm isn't restricted to what people might say or do. The harm can be spiritual. It can come from within. The word أَدْنَىٰ also means "more honored," so there's a recognition and honor that comes from Allah ﷻ Himself.

When someone argues that removing Islamic dress prevents harm, they must weigh what Allah ﷻ says. The harm of disobeying this command is guaranteed, whether or not people say anything negative. Pain will come from removing what Allah ﷻ has commanded, regardless of how society reacts.

However, we must be sensitive to the reality that observing hijab and jilbab is a genuine struggle for many women. We cannot be insensitive to sisters who are at different stages of understanding, conviction and practice. The law is the law—we cannot water down what Allah ﷻ has revealed. But we also cannot judge individuals whose circumstances, backgrounds and struggles we don't fully understand.

Consider this: modesty and consciousness of Allah ﷻ have been taught since the beginning of revelation. We're now in the fifth year after the migration to Medina. Revelation has been coming down for approximately 18 years at this point, yet these specific laws about outer dress are only now being revealed. The best of Muslims, including the Mothers of the Believers (RA), did not have these specific commandments for 18 years. They observed modesty and shame, but these particular laws came later.

This teaches us that there is such a thing as modesty and God-consciousness outside these specific garments. But once the law came down, it became law. It's like salah—you can't say "pray when you feel ready." It's an obligation. Similarly, we cannot present a watered-down version of what Allah ﷻ commands. However, just because it's law doesn't give us the right to judge people harshly who are at different stages in their journey.

When these ayahs were revealed, for a woman to completely change her wardrobe and manner of dress overnight was remarkable. That level of submission and response is truly incredible. And we should never diminish that by being harsh with those who struggle.

When ayahs come down about men, don't deflect by saying "What about the women?" And when ayahs come down about women, don't deflect by saying "What about the men?" Each instruction deserves its full attention and implementation.

This surah honors women and deals with specific issues related to women. Allah ﷻ chose to place these potentially controversial matters in a surah that discusses modesty, barriers and protection. What are we learning? That modest clothing is one of the great barriers protecting modesty. However, even with the most modest clothing, if you don't have taqwa, the clothing becomes meaningless. You could dress in the most observant way possible but still have questionable character, and your outer appearance would mean nothing.

Our outer garments—whether beards, thobes, kufis for men, or hijabs and jilbabs for women—mean nothing without the inner quality of God-consciousness. But we also cannot say "All that matters is character, so these external laws are irrelevant." That would be like saying "Salah only counts when you have 100% khushu (humility and focus)." Prayer is law. The quality of your prayer is a work in progress. Similarly, this dress code is law. Our modesty and character are ongoing projects. Law is in its place—black and white, clear and non-negotiable. Character development is in its place—subjective, qualitative, constantly evolving.

The ayah concludes وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (And Allah ﷻ has always been Forgiving and Merciful). This divine mercy is always available to those who struggle, who make mistakes, who are on the journey toward full submission.

translation: If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease and those who spread rumors in al-Madinah do not cease, We will surely incite you against them; then they will not remain your neighbors therein except for a little
commentary:
۞ لَّئِن لَّمْ يَنتَهِ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ وَٱلْمُرْجِفُونَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ لَنُغْرِيَنَّكَ بِهِمْ ثُمَّ لَا يُجَاوِرُونَكَ فِيهَآ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease and those who spread rumors in Medina do not cease, We will surely incite you against them. The ayah presents a stern warning to three groups causing harm in the Muslim community.

The المُنَـٰفِقُونَ (hypocrites) are those who outwardly claim Islam but inwardly reject it. The phrase ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ (those in whose hearts is disease) refers to those with corrupted intentions—in this context, those who pursued inappropriate behavior with women, who looked for opportunities to harass or proposition women, who pushed boundaries between men and women.

The third group, ٱلْمُرْجِفُونَ (those who spread rumors), comes from the root ر-ج-ف, which means to cause an earthquake or tremor. These are people whose goal is to create controversy, to shake up the community, to cause instability through scandal and gossip.

Remember the slander of Aisha (RA) discussed earlier in this surah. That was a deliberate attempt to create an earthquake of controversy within the Muslim community. If these groups don't stop their harmful behavior—if they continue harassing women, creating scandals, and spreading rumors—then لَنُغْرِيَنَّكَ بِهِمْ (We will surely incite you against them).

The verb أَغْرَى means to set someone on a target, like releasing hunting animals to pursue and tear down prey. Allah ﷻ is saying He will use the Prophet ﷺ to hunt down these troublemakers. ثُمَّ لَا يُجَاوِرُونَكَ فِيهَآ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (Then they will not remain your neighbors therein except for a little while). They will be expelled from Medina.

This is Allah's anger against those who try to instill shamelessness, controversy and scandal within the Muslim community. This particular threat was not carried out because the hypocrites restrained themselves enough to avoid this consequence. But the warning stands for all time.
