translation: Accursed wherever they are found, [being] seized and massacred completely
commentary:
مَّلْعُونِينَ ۖ أَيْنَمَا ثُقِفُوٓا۟ أُخِذُوا۟ وَقُتِّلُوا۟ تَقْتِيلًا

Accursed, wherever they are found they shall be seized and killed with a terrible slaughter. This continues the warning from the previous ayah. The intensity of the language—تَقْتِيلًا (killed with a thorough killing)—emphasizes the severity of Allah's position against those who corrupt the community from within.

translation: [This is] the established way of Allah with those who passed on before; and you will not find in the way of Allah any change
commentary:
سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ فِى ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ ٱللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا

This is the سُنَّة (established way) of Allah ﷻ concerning those who passed before, and you will never find any change in the sunnah of Allah ﷻ. Communities that engaged in these kinds of destructive behaviors were dealt with in the past, and Allah's way of dealing with such corruption doesn't change. This is a consistent pattern throughout history.

translation: People ask you concerning the Hour. Say," Knowledge of it is only with Allah. And what may make you perceive? Perhaps the Hour is near
commentary:
يَسْـَٔلُكَ ٱلنَّاسُ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ تَكُونُ قَرِيبًا

The transition in this ayah is striking. After discussing the protection of Medina and Muslim women, suddenly: People ask you about the Hour. Why this shift?

Because at the heart of all these commands—protecting the city, observing modesty, maintaining proper boundaries between men and women—is belief in the Last Day. Who will take these matters seriously? Only those who are conscious of standing before Allah ﷻ. Who will cover themselves properly today, whether men or women? Those who understand that on Judgment Day, there is no covering. We will stand before Allah ﷻ as we were born, completely exposed.

If we fear the humiliation of that Day and want to be covered and given shade, then we must get our affairs in order now. Tell them: Its knowledge is only with Allah ﷻ. وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ تَكُونُ قَرِيبًا (And what may make you perceive? Perhaps the Hour is near). This creates urgency. Don't delay your repentance. Don't delay your submission. The Hour might be closer than you think.

translation: Indeed, Allah has cursed the disbelievers and prepared for them a Blaze
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ سَعِيرًا

Indeed, Allah ﷻ has cursed the disbelievers and prepared for them a Blaze. This is the consequence for those who reject faith entirely—they face both the curse and the fire.

translation: Abiding therein forever, they will not find a protector or a helper
commentary:
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ لَّا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا

Abiding therein forever. They will find neither protector nor helper. The permanence is emphasized—خَـٰلِدِينَ (dwelling permanently) أَبَدًا (forever). When they need someone to protect them or help them against their punishment, they will find no one.

translation: The Day their faces will be turned about in the Fire, they will say, "How we wish we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Messenger
commentary:
يَوْمَ تُقَلَّبُ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِى ٱلنَّارِ يَقُولُونَ يَـٰلَيْتَنَآ أَطَعْنَا ٱللَّهَ وَأَطَعْنَا ٱلرَّسُولَا۠

On the Day their faces will be turned about in the Fire. This ayah has a parallel earlier in the surah. Remember in Surah Al-Ahzab, Allah ﷻ described the Day يَوْمَ تُقَلَّبُ ٱلْقُلُوبُ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ (the Day when hearts and eyes will turn) (33:10). Now we see يَوْمَ تُقَلَّبُ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِى ٱلنَّارِ (the Day their faces will be turned in the Fire). The imagery is of meat being turned as it cooks—their faces will be rotated in the flames.

يَقُولُونَ يَـٰلَيْتَنَآ أَطَعْنَا ٱللَّهَ وَأَطَعْنَا ٱلرَّسُولَا۠ (They will say, "Oh, if only we had obeyed Allah ﷻ and obeyed the Messenger ﷺ"). Notice the elongated form of the words, suggesting their screaming in agony. They extend the word الرَّسُولَا۠ as they cry out in regret.

translation: And they will say, "Our Lord, indeed we obeyed our masters and our dignitaries, and they led us astray from the [right] way
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّآ أَطَعْنَا سَادَتَنَا وَكُبَرَآءَنَا فَأَضَلُّونَا ٱلسَّبِيلَا۠

They will say, "Our Lord, indeed we obeyed our masters and our dignitaries, and they led us astray from the way." They blame their leaders—both their سَادَة (masters, chiefs) and their كُبَرَاء (great ones, elites). "We just followed what they told us. They're the ones who misled us."

Again, notice the elongated الْسَّبِيلَا۠ as they scream the word "the path" in their anguish.

translation: Our Lord, give them double the punishment and curse them with a great curse
commentary:
رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِهِمْ ضِعْفَيْنِ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَٱلْعَنْهُمْ لَعْنًا كَبِيرًا

"Our Lord, give them double the punishment and curse them with a great curse." They ask for their leaders to receive twice the punishment. Why should leaders get double punishment? Because their actions have consequences beyond themselves. When someone in leadership does wrong, the effects ripple outward.

This connects back to what the Mothers of the Believers were told earlier in the surah: If you do wrong, you'll receive double the punishment, and if you do right, you'll receive double the reward. Why? Because they're role models. Leaders are accountable not just for their own actions but for how those actions influence others.

This principle applies to all of us. Whether we accept it or not, as Muslims living in non-Muslim societies, we are ambassadors of Islam. At the elevator, the cash register, in traffic, at school, at work, at the airport—we represent Islam. When we do something wrong, people don't just judge us; they judge Islam. We're in a position of responsibility. Our bad behavior qualifies us for double punishment because we've made Islam look bad. This is serious.

translation: O you who have believed, be not like those who abused Moses; then Allah cleared him of what they said. And he, in the sight of Allah, was distinguished
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاذَوْا۟ مُوسَىٰ فَبَرَّأَهُ ٱللَّهُ مِمَّا قَالُوا۟ ۚ وَكَانَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَجِيهًا

O you who have believed, do not be like those who abused Musa (AS). The followers of Musa (AS) repeatedly questioned his instructions, played around with his commands, and caused him pain through their defiance. "What kind of cow do you mean? What color? What size?" They made matters difficult when Allah ﷻ had made things simple.

Now the Muslims are being told: Don't be like the people who caused Musa (AS) pain. Don't cause your Messenger ﷺ pain. In this surah, social laws have been revealed—how to seek permission before entering, how to show respect and dignity to the Mothers of the Believers, and the dress code for Muslim women. The connection is clear: when you don't care about these instructions, when you don't observe them in your own family or for yourself, you're being like those who caused Musa (AS) pain. But now you're causing pain to your own Prophet ﷺ.

فَبَرَّأَهُ ٱللَّهُ مِمَّا قَالُوا۟ (But Allah ﷻ cleared him of what they said). Allah ﷻ absolved Musa (AS) from the hurtful accusations. وَكَانَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَجِيهًا (And he was noble in the sight of Allah ﷻ). Despite what they said about him, despite how they treated him, his nobility before Allah ﷻ was unaffected.

The lesson: Don't misinterpret the teachings of the prophets and attribute to them what they never said or taught.

translation: O you who have believed, fear Allah and speak words of appropriate justice
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا

O you who have believed, be conscious of Allah ﷻ and speak قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا (words of appropriate justice). This ayah deserves careful attention because سَدِيدًا is a powerful and multifaceted word.

The root س-د-د means a protective wall, a preventative barrier. The large wall around a city that you cannot scale is called a سَدّ. In modern Arabic, a dam is called a سَدّ. In ancient Arabic, it referred to any protective barrier that prevents something from passing through—like a plug that stops a hole in a wall.

When Allah ﷻ commands قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا, He's commanding speech that is like a سَدّ—speech that prevents problems rather than creating them. This is speech that is clear, without confusion or double meanings. It's speech that solves problems rather than generating them.

Consider an example: You say "I'm so glad you're home." But the way you say it could mean you're genuinely happy, or it could be dripping with sarcasm. If your words can be taken multiple ways, that's not قَوْل سَدِيد. Preventative speech is clear speech that doesn't leave room for misunderstanding.

In marriage, this principle is crucial. After all the instructions about beautifying your marriages and observing proper conduct, we're now told: Don't let your marriage become ugly through speech that is not سَدِيد. Don't say things that can be taken the wrong way. Don't leave statements hanging, forcing your spouse to guess what you mean. Don't assume your partner understands what you're thinking. Spell things out.

When your husband says "I thought you were angry," don't respond with "Well, you should have known." You should have been clear. The same applies to husbands—be explicit. "I'll be home at five" is different from "I'll be home at five, I'm really hungry, should I pick something up or will you have food ready?" Clear communication prevents conflict.

Women often have high emotional intelligence—they can read how other women are feeling from subtle cues. But they sometimes assume their brothers, fathers, husbands or sons have the same intuition. We don't. We cannot figure things out from your facial expressions. If you look upset, we might just think you look like you just woke up. We don't know.

The scenario plays out repeatedly: A husband asks, "My friends are going out for iftar, can I join them?" The wife says, "Go, go, go." "Are you sure?" "Yes, go!" He goes, has a great time, and comes home to find her crying. "What happened?" "You never have iftar with me!" "But you said go!" "You should have seen my face."

This is not قَوْل سَدِيد from either party. If you're sure, say you're sure. If you're not sure, say you're not. And if someone says something that could be taken multiple ways, ask respectfully: "I don't want to misunderstand. Can you explain what you meant by that?" Most of the time, you'll discover it wasn't what you thought at all.

Clarification helps, but it must come from a place of understanding, not anger. The purpose is to ensure all speech is clear. Don't say things inside your head and expect others to hear them. Don't mumble responses. Don't be passive-aggressive. Be direct, honest and clear in your communication.

The first thing to die in a marriage is often communication. When conversations become generic—"How was your day?" "Fine." "How was yours?" "Good."—the relationship is already in trouble. Generic language means you've stopped really communicating.

This ayah is deep and comprehensive. It's not coincidental that it's one of the most emphasized ayahs about marriage. If you can maintain clear, honest, preventative communication, Allah ﷻ promises a beautiful result.

translation: He will [then] amend for you your deeds and forgive you your sins. And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly attained a great attainment
commentary:
يُصْلِحْ لَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ ۗ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَقَدْ فَازَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا

He will amend for you your deeds and forgive you your sins. If you can communicate properly with each other—with clear, honest, preventative speech—Allah ﷻ will fix your affairs. He'll set your matters right. Your issues will work out.

وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ (And forgive you your sins). He'll overlook your mistakes, cover your faults. وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَقَدْ فَازَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا (And whoever obeys Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ has certainly attained a great success). This is the ultimate success—combining obedience to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ in all matters, including how we speak to each other.

## Ayahs 72-73

إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا ٱلْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلْجِبَالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَن يَحْمِلْنَهَا وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا وَحَمَلَهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ ظَلُومًا جَهُولًا • لِّيُعَذِّبَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ وَيَتُوبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًۢا

Indeed, We offered the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, but they refused to undertake it and were apprehensive of it. But man undertook it. Indeed, he was unjust and ignorant.

What is this أَمَانَة (Trust)? According to some scholars, this Trust is the human soul itself—the ruh that has the ability to make choices. This is what makes human beings extraordinary. وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى فَقَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ سَـٰجِدِينَ (And I breathed into him of My spirit, so fall down in prostration to him) (15:29). This ruh, this spirit that Allah ﷻ created and placed inside us, is the great Trust.

Another interpretation is that the Trust is the Quran itself. There's a parallel ayah later in the Quran: لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَّرَأَيْتَهُۥ خَـٰشِعًا مُّتَصَدِّعًا مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ (If We had sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled and splitting apart from fear of Allah ﷻ) (59:21). The Quran is received by the ruh of Muhammad ﷺ. It comes from one ruh (Jibreel AS) to another ruh (the Prophet's ﷺ). That is the Trust.

We presented this Trust to the skies, the earth and the mountains, and فَأَبَيْنَ أَن يَحْمِلْنَهَا (they refused to undertake it). When we say "this table refused to hold the weight," we don't mean the table consciously declined. We mean it couldn't handle the burden and collapsed. Similarly, the heavens, earth and mountains couldn't possibly bear this burden. If the Quran came down on a mountain, you would see it shatter and explode from the fear of Allah ﷻ.

وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا (And they were apprehensive of it)—they were overwhelmed with fear at the possibility of this burden. وَحَمَلَهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ (But man undertook it). The human being—this forgetful creature—carried that burden.

Think about this: The Quran comes down on a mountain and the mountain would shatter. But a believer recites Quran while playing video games, and nothing happens in his heart. It's all good. He can keep reciting while distracted, unmoved, unaffected. How hard must that heart be? Clearly harder than a mountain, because the mountain would have shattered, but this believer's heart remains unaffected.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ ظَلُومًا جَهُولًا (Indeed, he was unjust and ignorant). The human being is an extreme wrong-doer and جَهُولًا —overwhelmed by emotion, impulsive. Jahool describes someone who just follows whatever his whims say, acting on impulse without thought.

But here's the crucial point: When Allah ﷻ gave this Trust to the human being, it means the human being has the potential to carry it. He has the power to do justice to revelation. The fact that many humans fail to honor this Trust doesn't negate the potential within every human soul.

لِّيُعَذِّبَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ (So that Allah ﷻ may punish the hypocrite men and hypocrite women and the idolatrous men and idolatrous women). Notice that men and women are highlighted on both sides—believers and disbelievers. This surah began with addressing both, and it ends with addressing both.

وَيَتُوبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ (But that Allah ﷻ may accept repentance from the believing men and believing women). What are we learning? When the ayahs of the Quran truly take hold of your heart, when you hear them and they grab you, what should you do? Make tawbah.

The ayahs are supposed to compel us to repent. You're reading ayahs about marriage, about speech, about modesty. You just had a fight with your spouse. You hear قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا and you immediately think: I need to make tawbah to Allah ﷻ. That's what these ayahs are for—so that Allah ﷻ may accept the repentance of believing men and believing women.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًۢا (And Allah ﷻ has always been Forgiving and Merciful). At the end of the day, your forgiveness and your access to His mercy is tied to your relationship with this Trust. This incredible Book that has been handed to you, and this soul that can carry the message of this Book—realize the gift you've been given.

May Allah ﷻ help us become of those who do justice to His Book and hold this Trust as it's supposed to be held. May He make us worthy carriers of His revelation.

translation: Indeed, we offered the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, and they declined to bear it and feared it; but man [undertook to] bear it. Indeed, he was unjust and ignorant

[It was] so that Allah may punish the hypocrite men and hypocrite women and the men and women who associate others with Him and that Allah may accept repentance from the believing men and believing women. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful
